# CHAPTER 1: MISSING  
*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T2*

The cot was empty.

Tiffani stopped in the doorway with her slate braced against her forearm and the chalk already poised. She had a three-Tide plan for him. Breathing rate. Tremor amplitude. Pupil response. Autonomic drift. Everything that would establish a usable baseline for Stage Four progression before the body started cutting corners she couldn’t document back into order.

The sheets weren’t shoved aside.

They were folded. Corner to corner. Tight. Clean.

A paralyzed man did not fold sheets.

She stepped in and let her eyes do what they did best: turn a room into counts. Cold stone underfoot. No grit under her boots. No scuff line where a heel dragged.

Cup on the side table—placed at arm’s reach from where his shoulder would have been. He couldn’t have reached it yesterday. The calibration strap was gone. So was his secondary slate. Three pieces of copper-stained chalk, the ones he kept separate because the residue read cleaner when you weren’t cross-contaminating with common white.

His primary measurement slate remained.

The heavy one.

The one that mattered.

She wrote, neat and uniform:

*Cot empty. Sheets folded. Cup repositioned. Tools taken: calibration strap, secondary slate, chalk (3 pcs, copper-stained). Tools left: primary slate.*

Her hand stayed steady. She kept her eyes on the slate while she wrote. Looking at absence invited speculation. Speculation wasn’t data.

Footsteps halted in the corridor. The latch clicked.

A medical clerk edged into the doorway—young, trying to look like his spine had been issued with the uniform.

“The director wants to see you. About records.”

“After.” Tiffani didn’t turn. “Explain the patient evacuation process.”

“There wasn’t one.” His mouth tightened at one corner. “No evacuation authorization filed. No transfer order. No discharge.”

Tiffani’s chalk paused.

“Then he’s still documented as admitted.”

“That’s the problem.” The clerk swallowed. Once, then again. “Someone removed the admission ledger page. The director is… unclear what protocol is, now.”

Tiffani wrote, smaller, where it wouldn’t be the first thing read if her slate was seized:

*Institutional obstruction: logged.*

The clerk left fast, as if speed could become innocence.

She waited six heartbeats—enough time for panic to get down the hall and start infecting other people—then went to the guard.

He was posted outside the door. Young. Fresh uniform. New fabric still holding its fold.

“Patient Jhace Torrins,” she said. “Stage Four. Admitted A4-T19. Paralysis from sternum down. Motor function nonexistent. When did he leave?”

The guard snapped straighter. “He didn’t. No one entered. No one left. I’ve been here since second bell.”

“The door was watched the entire time.”

“Yes, ma’am.”

“No breaks. No distractions. No message that required you to look away.”

His eyes flicked once—down the corridor, then back. His fingers tightened on the door frame. “I checked the corridor. Standard sweep. Maybe forty heartbeats.”

“Forty,” Tiffani said, and wrote it down.

While her chalk moved, she watched him the way she watched structures under load. Breath shallow. Pupils wide for a corridor that bright. Knuckles pale.

He was either lying.

Or he’d seen something he didn’t have language for and had decided silence was safer than truth.

She didn’t press. Pressure changed behavior. Changed behavior ruined reads.

“Thank you,” she said, and left him with his fear.

Back inside, she shut the door and stood in the space where the impossible had happened.

The grid reference-hum in this wing carried a second mode. It sat under the normal vibration in the stone—lower, deeper, persistent. She’d noticed it yesterday. She hadn’t written it because noticing wasn’t the same as confirming.

Copper rose behind her tongue. Stronger than yesterday.

Her fingers twitched once as she lifted the chalk.

She did not add that line to the slate.

If he moved, there was a vector. If someone moved him, there was evidence. Her job was to make the room confess without asking it politely.

She started at the threshold and worked clockwise.

Doorframe: no fresh marks. Wall: no shoulder smear, no cloth rub, no grit ground into plaster at carry height. Floor: no drag line. Window: latched, dust on the sill undisturbed.

The ash bin by the cold stove held half-burned paper. She knelt and sifted with two fingers. Most of it collapsed into black powder. One piece held long enough to read:

*…locked maintenance corridor…requires two hands + walking…impossible…*

A report.

Someone had written it.

Someone had decided it needed to vanish.

She slid the scrap into her notes.

Footsteps again. Faster this time. Not careful.

Bram appeared in the doorway, coat rumpled. His face tried to assemble a joke and quit halfway.

“Cool,” he said, staring at the empty cot. “Love walking into rooms where the paralyzed guy quits his job and leaves.”

“Not left.” Tiffani stood. Ash dust clung to her fingertips; she wiped it onto her trousers without looking. “Moved. Method unknown.”

Bram’s jaw worked. “That is not better.”

“It’s accurate.”

He dropped onto a crate beside the cot. The wood complained. “Tiff. Yesterday he couldn’t move his fingers. Today he’s gone. That’s not clerical.”

“No. It’s authority.” She kept her voice level because anger was loud and the building liked to listen. “Someone with reach removed a ledger page and burned a report.”

She lifted Jhace’s primary slate—tested the weight, the worn edge where his thumb always rode. No note. No instruction. Just deliberate placement.

A control variable left behind.

She set it back exactly where it had been.

Bram watched her do it. “So what now?”

“Now I follow physics,” Tiffani said. “Residue. Mode surges. Stress anomalies. He didn’t evaporate. He left a trail or someone else did.”

“And if he doesn’t want to be found?”

She didn’t answer. Want was not a measurable property. Her method didn’t have a column for will.

She packed instead. Slate. Chalk. Scrap. Calipers. The small wedge gauge she used when she needed the world to admit angles.

Bram stood. “You’re going to get us into something bad.”

“Define bad.”

“The kind where we disappear and somebody burns our paperwork too.”

Tiffani closed the kit. The buckles snapped, clean and final. “Then we document faster than they can destroy.”

They left the room together. The guard remained at his post. She didn’t look at him.

Seven incidents in three Tides. Seven holds that should have failed. Copper-gold residue where it didn’t belong. A second mode in the hum that followed a pattern.

Someone was working without paying the visible cost markers.

Someone was leaving proof.

Tiffani started down the stairs. Her hand trembled once against the rail.

Her right knee caught on the third step, locked, then released.

She kept walking.

She left both facts off the slate.

---

The building took a hit.

Not a crack of impact—no sharp report, no ringing. A low, muffled bass punch that arrived through the soles of her boots and climbed her shins into her teeth. The stairwell flexed. Dust sifted from a seam overhead. Lamps swung on their chains, lazy at first, then wider.

Bram froze mid-step, one hand lifted like it could steady the air.

Tiffani stopped breathing long enough to listen with her whole body.

The grid reference-hum stuttered. The second mode swelled hard, then dipped, then settled into something heavier.

“What was—” Bram started.

“Quiet,” Tiffani said.

They stood while the city decided whether it was done being violent.

For four heartbeats the only sound was lamp-chain creak and distant shouting that hadn’t learned the new emergency yet.

Then came pressure. Not impact. Weight. A broad shove that made the stairwell door tremble in its frame.

The hinges squealed.

The seam under the door darkened.

Water forced itself through first as a line, then as fingers, then as a palm. Cold. Salt-sharp. Laced with silt. It smelled of sea rot and crushed algae and harbor sludge scraped up from below.

Bram backed up. “No. No, no—”

The door bucked. The frame split at a crack that had been there before today and had just been given a reason to open. Water punched through in a sheet and hit the stairwell wall hard enough to throw spray across the stone.

Glass shattered somewhere above them—one of the narrow corridor windows giving up. The next surge came through that opening and down the stairs in a grinding rush that carried splinters, rope ends, and a plank spinning end-over-end.

People screamed on the landing below. The sound cut short as the water found them.

Tiffani grabbed Bram by the collar and yanked him up, away from the stairwell mouth, toward higher steps. Her kit slammed her hip with each movement. Cold brine licked at the treads behind them.

Saint Merro had been built to keep the sea outside.

The sea had revised the plan.

The Brinehammer was in the streets now, and the city’s stonework was about to learn what force looked like when it didn’t care about authority, ledgers, or locks.

---

# CHAPTER 2: FORM  
*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T2*

The room smelled of chalk dust and old stone.

Corlexi sat at a narrow table that served two lives. One life was grain tallies, transport costs, and the harmless boredom of a merchant. The other was pinned to the walls in colored ink and metal points: incident maps, cost progression curves, termination thresholds, name lists compiled without consent because consent was a luxury the Crown didn’t bother buying.

A knock came—wrong timing, wrong rhythm.

He moved without haste. Charts slid under a merchant ledger. His shoulders settled into the posture that invited no second look.

An older woman stood in the doorway. Textile calluses. Eyes that didn’t waste motion.

“Message from Salee,” she said. “She says you’re documenting the Dregs.”

“I document inventory,” Corlexi replied. “Documentation keeps business from bleeding.”

“Salee says documentation is business.” The woman’s gaze stayed fixed on his face. “She wanted you to know she sees you.”

She left before he could waste a response.

Corlexi waited until the footfall faded, then unbuttoned his shirt to the waist.

The scars cut diagonal across his torso—pale seams where copper-gold had fused into tissue during the Piercing. Twelve fragments, each no larger than a thumbnail. He had counted them enough times that the number lived behind his eyes.

He took a breath and tested.

No tremor.

No exhaustion drift.

No vein-lines crawling up his forearms.

He focused on the crooked lamp bracket across the room. He read it. Iron. Stress at the joint where the mount met the wall. Load tolerance still adequate, but the fasteners had been giving way by degrees for at least two Anneals.

He adjusted the structure.

The bracket straightened with a soft click and settled as if it had never been wrong.

Form. Structure maintained.

No visible filament light. No copper spill. No cost marker. The room stayed quiet except for chain sway.

That absence made him valuable.

It also made him a problem that could be solved.

He buttoned up and pulled the collapse reports back into the open.

Seven incidents in three Tides. Saint Merro took most of them—still bleeding from the Piercing, still trying to pretend the grid reference-hum was “normal” again. But the holds were wrong. Too clean. Too complete. Casualty projections that should have been met—and weren’t. Residue concentrated in ways standard dispersal didn’t produce.

Someone else was operating outside the visible cost curve.

He wrote on his slate, not as speculation but as a working risk:

*Pattern suggests internal shard integration may suppress standard cost markers. Requires confirmation. Additional subjects needed.*

A faint vibration rolled through the wall. Not ambient drift. Something low and distant, like stone being informed that it might have to move. He didn’t look at the window. Looking was a tell.

The hallway outside filled with hurried feet. Not panicked—directed. Directed movement meant a network, and networks meant Salee.

A second knock, harder.

Corlexi swept the charts under the ledger again and opened the door to find a runner with salt on his cuffs and a textile knot tied to his wrist.

“Saint Merro got hit,” the runner said. “Water in the streets. People moving. Refugees coming.”

Corlexi let his face stay dull, merchant-appropriate. “Why tell me.”

“Because you write things down,” the runner said. “And because writing means planning, and planning means we don’t die stupid.”

The runner shoved a folded list into Corlexi’s hand. Not names. Needs. Rope. Boards. Dry cloth. Nails. Bread. Something to brace doors that had stopped being doors.

Corlexi looked at the list and felt, for the first time, the registry become useful in the way knives were useful. Not moral. Practical.

He put the list under the grain ledger, then asked, “Where’s Salee.”

“Routing the first wave,” the runner said. “Also—your building is taking load.”

“Load.”

“People,” the runner snapped. “Too many. Too fast. Floors aren’t meant for it.”

The runner left. Corlexi stood in the doorway and listened to his own building.

Stone carried the mass clean. Wood carried it late. The disagreement made small noises he’d ignored for months. Now it mattered.

He moved down the stairs and into the lower room where residents were already packing. Not a formal shelter—just a place people knew would still have a roof when the Dregs’ patched structures gave out.

A woman with brine-stiff hair shoved a bundle under a table. A man hauled a child up onto a bench. Someone slammed the door and the frame answered with a complaint.

Corlexi saw it: the central beam had a hairline split at the join, old damage made worse by the sudden shift in weight. It would hold another hour. Maybe two. Then it would let go and take a corner of the floor with it.

If it dropped, people would panic. Panic turned into uncontrolled coupling. Uncontrolled coupling turned into bodies.

He could intervene.

He could also be seen.

Salee’s people were in this room. He recognized the quiet ones: eyes that tracked exits, hands that touched beams without looking like they were checking them.

Corlexi moved like a merchant checking inventory. He set down a crate, leaned, and let his focus slide into the split join.

Form. Quiet. Surgical.

He didn’t “fix” it. He redistributed load just enough to buy time. The split stopped widening. The beam’s vibration steadied.

Cost: none visible.

Risk: immediate.

A man near the wall turned his head sharply, as if he’d heard something that wasn’t a sound. The runner with the textile knot looked at Corlexi’s hands.

Corlexi straightened, picked up a sack of grain, and made a show of irritation. “If you’re stacking bodies in here, stack them against the stone wall, not mid-span. You want the floor to keep being a floor.”

The instruction sounded like merchant complaint.

It was also correct.

People shifted where they stood. The load redistributed again, now by human choice. That mattered. It meant his adjustment would be blamed on common sense, not on anomaly.

The runner nodded once, almost imperceptible, and moved on.

Corlexi returned upstairs and wrote a single line on a merchant sheet:

*Saint Merro inundation event initiated. Dregs taking refugee load. Structural failure risk increases. Cover risk increases with emergency intervention.*

Then, smaller, where it could hide:

*Salee sees me. Now her people may have felt me.*

He folded the page under grain totals and resumed the posture of a man who cared only about prices.

---

# CHAPTER 3: WAKE  
*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T3 through A5-T7*

Seven reports. Seven incidents. Three Tides.

Tiffani laid them across her workspace and didn’t let herself blink too long. Patterns didn’t reveal themselves to people who looked away.

Seam collapse near Junction Twelve boundary. Estimated hold duration: two hundred heartbeats. Zero casualties where her structural projection demanded four minimum. Copper-gold residue concentrated at load junctions instead of fanning out through surrounding material.

Foundation stabilization at Merchant Row—same residue behavior.

Bridge support failure at the southern canal—again.

Each site carried a mode surge in the grid reference-hum that didn’t match ambient drift. Not random noise. A signature.

She plotted locations on a city map and drew lines with a tool made for straight truth. The geometry didn’t argue.

The vector pointed northeast.

Grindheim.

A quiet scrape at her door. Not a knock. Someone trying not to be recorded by sound.

Corran stepped in and shut it behind him. Mid-level Guild administration. Tired eyes. Restricted stamp on the folder in his hand. The expression of a man walking into a decision he already hated.

“Unofficially,” he said, “you did not get this from me.”

He set the folder down and didn’t sit.

“The director is calling it clerical loss,” Corran continued. “Lost ledger pages. Misfiled incident routing. Your search authorization gets revoked next Tide.”

Tiffani stood and went to the archive cabinet on instinct. Pulled the Saint Merro incident drawer.

It didn’t open.

Not stuck—locked. Fresh metal. New keyway.

Corran didn’t look at the lock. He stared at the floor like the floor might offer forgiveness.

“They moved fast,” Tiffani said.

“They moved before I could stop them,” Corran replied.

Tiffani let her fingers rest on the new lock just long enough to feel the cold. Then she stepped back.

“Good,” she said. “Now it’s provable.”

Corran’s throat worked. “Someone doesn’t want you following this.”

Tiffani opened the folder.

Eighth incident. Grindheim Dregs. Old textile district. Same residue concentration. Same hold cleanliness. Same missing casualties.

Her chalk hovered over her slate.

“Why help me?” she asked.

Corran’s voice dropped. “Because your ledger page wasn’t ‘lost.’ It was removed. I saw the request in the archive before it got scrubbed. Administrative channel. Crown-level reach.”

Institutional obstruction: confirmed.

Institutional interference: resourced.

Tiffani kept her face still. “What do you want.”

“When you understand what happened—when you find out what was done to him—send word outside the official channels.” Corran didn’t offer a method. He didn’t need to. “Don’t send it where they can erase it.”

He left without waiting for agreement. That, too, was a data point.

Two Tides later she found Bram in the Guild hall with a chipped cup and something dark inside it.

“I need access to Grindheim,” she said. “Before the revocation lands. You have textile ties and harbor adjacency. Get me a reason that opens doors.”

Bram looked up like his body wanted to make a joke and his brain filed a denial. “I’m not a spy. I’m barely functional.”

“You can move where I can’t,” she said. “And people talk when you make them laugh.”

He exhaled. “That is… accurate.”

“Get me in.”

His cup hit the table once, hard enough to rattle the saucer. “When this goes bad, I’m haunting you. I’m going to move your chalk. I’m going to rearrange your kit. I’m going to make your straight lines crooked.”

Tiffani didn’t look up from her slate. “Accepted.”

She returned to her workspace and finished the map.

Eight incidents. Then nine, after another report slid to her through a quiet chain of hands that avoided stamps and signatures. The vector held. Grindheim’s Dregs. A place where buildings leaned because they had to, and information moved through people instead of paperwork.

As she drew the final line, copper surged behind her tongue.

Her right knee locked mid-motion, released, then refused to cooperate for six heartbeats.

She gripped the desk edge until function returned.

She did not write either fact down.

If she logged it, she lost the work. If she lost the work, he stayed missing. So she didn’t log it.

She packed her kit. Slate. Chalk. The burned scrap. The incident reports that existed outside official circulation because someone decided truth didn’t qualify as permitted knowledge.

Tomorrow they left for Grindheim.

Tomorrow she followed the physics trail into a district where institutions held no power and networks held all of it.

Tomorrow she became unauthorized on purpose.

At the bottom of the stairs outside her workspace, she paused and wrote one line in a personal margin she never showed anyone:

*Jhace chose departure. Authority chose concealment. Physics will decide who’s lying.*

Her hand shook while she wrote it.

She still left the tremor off the slate.

---

# CHAPTER 4: SEARCHING  
*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T3*

He woke expecting paralysis.

His fingers moved.

Jhace lay still and ran the test in the only order that ever kept him alive: smallest system first, then expand.

Right hand: functional. Delay of three heartbeats between intention and motion.  
Left hand: functional. Same delay.  
Toes: responsive, but the signal arrived late, as if the line had been rerouted through bad junctions.  
Legs: sensation present. Not remembered—present.

The tremor was gone.

He raised both hands into the thin light and watched them. Steady. No shake in the knuckles. No flutter at the wrist.

That should have felt like relief.

It didn’t.

Something else sat in its place—heat behind his sternum, constant and wrong. Not fever. Not pain. Pressure, like a brace tightened one notch too far and left there.

He sat up.

The room answered with a soft tilt that corrected itself a heartbeat late. His body was lagging behind his own commands. His limbs moved as if they were accepting delayed instructions. He didn’t fight it. Fighting made mistakes.

He looked around.

Not the infirmary.

A narrow maintenance room with a cold pipe run along one wall and a low steel table bolted to the floor. Dust in the corners. Chalk scuffs on stone. Someone had used this place for work and then stopped.

His secondary slate lay on the table. His calibration strap beside it. Three copper-stained chalk pieces laid in a line, tips aligned. Not dumped. Set.

His primary slate was missing.

He remembered leaving it behind. The heavy one stayed in the room where he’d been officially “admitted,” because the heavy one would become evidence in someone else’s hands.

He looked at his palm.

Copper-gold light sat under the skin in thin threads that weren’t veins and weren’t ink. When he flexed, it brightened. When he stopped, it dimmed but didn’t disappear.

Memory came in broken strips.

The cot. Unable to move. Unable to speak. Breathing and watching and waiting for the body to betray him completely.

A figure in the doorway.

Not shadowed. Not lit. Light fell wrong around it. Edges without behavior.

The burn in his palm.

The feeling of something being drawn through him—every nerve, every vessel, every joint—surveyed and set.

He couldn’t remember a face.

He couldn’t remember a voice.

He couldn’t remember why.

Only the certainty that what happened to him wasn’t a rescue.

It was a procedure.

A low vibration rolled through the building. Not the normal grid reference-hum. Something heavier, slow enough to make the pipe run above him shiver.

Then came the sound: a deep, muffled bass strike, distant but clean through stone.

He held his breath. Counted. Listened.

Pressure followed. Broad. The table legs squealed against their bolts. Dust dropped in a thin curtain from a seam in the ceiling.

He stood too fast. The lag punished him; his legs accepted the command late and he had to catch himself on the pipe.

Heat surged behind his sternum. Copper flooded his mouth.

He tasted brine in it.

He pulled his kit to him and strapped it on by muscle memory. The buckles clicked. The strap bit his shoulder. Good. Real.

He wrote one line on the secondary slate:

*A5-T3. Mobility restored. Tremor absent. Phase lag present. Core pressure constant.*

He tested coupling.

He didn’t want to. He did it anyway because ignorance killed faster than cost.

He focused on the pipe bracket.

The coupling didn’t start in his hands.

It started behind his sternum. A tight bloom that pushed outward. Light didn’t spill from his fingers; it bled through his ribs into the metal, into the wall, into the stone underfoot. He felt the stress in the bracket joint without touching it. He felt how close it was to slipping.

He pushed.

The bracket settled. The vibration in the pipe smoothed. The structure complied.

Heat surged hard enough to blur his vision at the edges. Clean lines softened into something less reliable.

He broke coupling fast.

The room stayed upright. He stayed upright.

Cost, then: heat. Latency. Core-origin coupling. Tremor replaced.

He wrote:

*Coupling origin shifted (sternum/core). Cost markers: heat surge + visual edge loss + phase lag worsens post-hold.*

Water hit somewhere above him. A slap of brine against stone. Then the sound of glass giving up—one pane, then another—followed by shouting that cut short too quickly.

Jhace’s hand clenched on the chalk until the tip snapped.

He didn’t go up.

Going up meant returning to the ledger that had lost a page with his name on it. Going up meant “recovery” under people who had watched him disappear without sounding an alarm.

Mobel surfaced in his mind, sharp and immediate.

The other shard-bearer. The one who held without visible cost markers. The one who walked away from failure as if failure didn’t apply.

If anyone had language for integration, it was Mobel.

Jhace took what he needed and left by the maintenance corridor the burned report had named—locked, requiring two hands and walking.

He had both hands.

And he could walk.

That was the only impossible part of today that mattered.

---

# CHAPTER 5: AUTHORITY  
*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T3*

News didn’t arrive in the Dregs as proclamations.

It arrived as pressure.

Corlexi was midway through a termination-threshold recalculation when the building’s vibration changed. The grid reference-hum carried a low shove under its normal band. Stone transmitted it cleanly. Wood transmitted it late. The disagreement set his teeth on edge.

A messenger came ten minutes later.

Not a Crown courier. Not a Guild runner. A boy with a textile knot tied to his wrist and salt on his sleeves.

He didn’t step fully into the room. He didn’t look at the walls.

“Saint Merro got hit,” the boy said.

“With what?” Corlexi asked, though he already knew the shape of it from the vibration.

The boy’s throat worked. “Sea came into the streets. Doors blew. Windows blew. People dragged out like scrap.”

Corlexi set his pencil down and kept his face neutral. He did not ask whether the infirmary stood. Curiosity looked like investment. Investment looked like knowledge. Knowledge got extracted.

“How many?” he asked instead.

The boy shrugged, too small for the math. “Enough.”

Corlexi slid a coin across the table. The boy took it and vanished.

Corlexi stared at the blank space left behind and felt the registry in his hands gain weight it hadn’t had yesterday.

Disaster did that. It turned “eventual” into “now.”

He rose, moved to the window, and watched the Dregs do what it always did when the wider world broke: redistribute load.

People were already moving along the streets with bundled cloth, water jugs, boards, rope. A woman shouted directions from a doorway with no sign and no guard. Two manifestors held a leaning walkway while someone hammered in a brace. A child ran messages and didn’t get stepped on.

Salee’s network was visible in the flow—not a person, a pattern. Routes cleared. Resources shifted. Witnesses repositioned. Every action aimed at one outcome: keep the Dregs from becoming the next place the sea chose to correct.

Then came the other wave.

Relief.

A Crown-marked wagon rolled into the district with a canvas banner and a soup pot strapped to the back. Behind it walked two clerks with clean cuffs and ink fingers. Behind them walked a pair of guards who looked like they’d been told “help” and meant “enforce.”

People watched from doorways. Nobody cheered. Nobody rushed the pot. The Dregs had learned long ago that offered food came with strings.

The lead clerk set a folding table in the street and laid down blank pages like he was planting flags.

“Displaced residents,” he called, voice practiced. “Relief allocation requires identification. Name and residence district. Manifestation status if known. Injuries noted. Supplies issued.”

A few people moved forward because hunger was persuasive.

A woman gave a name. The clerk wrote it, then slid her a ladle of thin broth.

Corlexi watched the exchange and made a calculation that didn’t require numbers:

Soup in exchange for a registry.

Crown ink moving through a disaster channel. Clean. Efficient. Hard to argue against if you liked staying alive.

Salee did not appear. That was its own signal. Her people would handle this without making her visible.

Corlexi returned to the table and pulled out a clean sheet under his merchant ledger.

He wrote:

*A5-T3. Saint Merro inundation event. Secondary effects: displacement + uncontrolled manifestation spikes + increased pressure for Crown registry implementation.*

He paused, then added a line he didn’t like writing:

*Enforcement disguised as relief.*

Outside, a manifestor in line tried to hold a buckling awning while also keeping a child from being shoved underfoot. Tremor in both arms. Stage Two pushing into Three. Her hold broke. The awning frame snapped down and clipped a man’s shoulder.

The crowd flinched and surged. Two people’s hands lit with copper-gold filaments in instinctive coupling. The light jumped to the awning supports, then stuttered. Both manifestors recoiled, pain in their faces, but the awning stayed up long enough for bodies to clear.

Uncontrolled coupling. Unmeasured cost. A perfect storm for Crown paperwork.

A guard stepped toward them.

Salee’s network stepped faster.

Someone shouted, “Brace left!” as if that was the only instruction needed. Someone else shoved a post into place. The crisis moved away from the clerks without ever becoming “an incident” on their pages.

Corlexi watched the guard’s eyes track the copper-gold afterglow on the supports.

He could have eased the load with Form and left no visible filament, no residue geometry.

He didn’t.

Not because he didn’t care.

Because if he intervened too cleanly, the wrong eyes would learn his pattern. The Dregs had eyes. Salee had eyes. And now, the Crown had sharpened its own.

He received a sealed note before noon.

Crown wax. Simple. Heavy.

He broke it and read:

**Directive:** accelerate compilation. Prioritize high-output manifestors. Prepare classification categories for “anomalous cost suppression” and “non-consensual stabilization events.” Assign liaisons to relief points.

Assign liaisons.

Meaning: embed the registry deeper, using hunger as leverage.

Corlexi folded the paper and slid it under grain invoices.

He wrote one last line where no one would look twice:

*Acceleration acknowledged. Cover risk increased. Salee aware. Relief point liaison will tighten scrutiny.*

He didn’t write the rest.

If Saint Merro’s ledgers were burning, the Crown would make new ones. It would use the Brinehammer as ink and call it safety.

---

# CHAPTER 6: DREGS  
*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T5*

Saint Merro didn’t recover. It redistributed damage.

Two Tides after the Brinehammer, the lower streets still smelled of salt rot and smashed kelp. The harbor quarter looked chewed—doors ripped from hinges, glass ground into mortar, boards jammed into cracks where the sea had punched through.

The infirmary remained standing. That didn’t mean it was functional.

It meant it was occupied.

Tiffani walked past triage cots lined in the corridor where Jhace had vanished. People lay wrapped in damp blankets with brine-cracked lips and eyes that refused to close. A manifestor shook uncontrollably while a healer held his shoulders down and told him to stop fighting his own nerves.

The records door was still sealed. The seal had been replaced once. Fresh wax over old wax.

Authority didn’t rebuild. It layered.

Bram waited for her at the side exit, hood up against rain that wasn’t rain—spray still carried inland on wind.

“You’re going to pretend this didn’t change everything?” he asked.

Tiffani didn’t slow. “It changed the environment. Not the methodology.”

Bram followed, splashing through shallow pooled brine in the alley. “That’s the kind of sentence people say right before they die for a principle.”

“Then they die consistent,” Tiffani said.

He made a sound that was almost a laugh and almost a choke. “You’re insane.”

“I’m correct,” she said, because saying it out loud helped keep her steady.

They reached her workspace. The door had been forced once and repaired badly. The lock caught. The frame sat slightly wrong. She noted it and didn’t write it down.

Inside, her incident map still hung where she’d left it. Eight pins. Then nine. Then the Brinehammer pins: where water broke through, where structures failed under saturation, where emergency holds had been thrown without documentation.

Her pattern didn’t collapse.

It sharpened.

Because now there was a second pressure vector: chaos.

Chaos made people manifest badly.

Bad manifestation left residue.

Residue didn’t lie.

She spread the reports she had—some from Corran, some pulled from exhausted foremen, some bought with favors.

A clear thread remained: clean holds, concentrated copper-gold geometry, no visible cost markers.

The same signature leading northeast.

Grindheim.

But now travel was restricted. Not by distance. By control.

At the gate office, an administrator with salt-stained cuffs looked at her travel request and didn’t bother pretending.

“Saint Merro is under stabilization protocol,” he said. “Movement out is limited to authorized relief crews.”

“I am an authorized methodist,” Tiffani said.

“Not anymore.” He tapped a stamp on his desk—Crown co-mark on Guild authority. New. Sharp. “Search permissions are suspended until further notice. We can’t spare you.”

You can’t spare your witness, she thought.

She didn’t say it.

She walked out and found Corran waiting in a side corridor like he’d been living there between regrets.

“They’re locking down,” he said quietly.

“I noticed.”

“They’re using the Brinehammer as cover,” Corran said. “People won’t question missing pages when they’re hauling bodies out of basements.”

Copper gathered behind Tiffani’s tongue. Not from the sea. From inside her.

“What’s your exit,” she asked.

Corran didn’t smile. “I don’t have one. You do.”

He handed her a folded pass—textile shipment escort, Grindheim-bound. Bram’s name on it. Not hers.

“Don’t,” Corran added, meaning don’t get caught, don’t make me responsible, don’t make me choose.

Tiffani took the pass. “Thank you,” she said, because it was accurate and because gratitude was a kind of receipt.

That night they left with a textile wagon crew hauling salvage cloth and salt-stiff rope toward Grindheim. The road north was broken where floodwater had undermined stone. They took detours across temporary bridges held by manifestors whose hands shook too hard to hold their own cups.

Tiffani watched the holds and forced herself not to correct them out loud. She wasn’t here to train. She was here to track.

By the time Grindheim’s Dregs rose into view—leaning buildings, stitched bridges, walls braced with whatever survived—her knee had locked and released so many times she’d started counting the intervals.

She still didn’t log it.

They found Salee by following motion.

A building with no sign and too much traffic. People moving in and out without hesitation. No guards. No ceremony. Just flow.

Inside, Salee stood near a table that wasn’t a desk so much as a routing surface. Objects placed with intent—chalk marks, string loops, a glass shard that caught light and threw it sideways.

“You’re the methodist,” Salee said.

“That’s a label,” Tiffani answered.

Salee’s eyes moved over her kit, her hands, her posture. “Labels tell me where to place you.”

“I’m looking for Jhace Torrins,” Tiffani said. “He vanished from Saint Merro under institutional suppression. His trail points here.”

Salee didn’t feign surprise. “Everything points here when the world breaks. Saint Merro got punched. Refugees will come. Authority will follow them. And you walked in with a kit full of order.”

Tiffani held her slate steady. “Order is how people survive.”

“People survive by adapting,” Salee said. “Order comes later, after the body count stops moving.”

Bram shifted beside Tiffani. Salee’s gaze flicked to him and back.

“You want my network,” Salee continued. “Witnesses. Routing. Safe passage. The Dregs doesn’t give that away.”

“What do you want,” Tiffani asked.

Salee tapped a piece of chalk on the table. Copper-stained. Not Tiffani’s. “Your protocols. How you map cost. How you decide who is about to die. My people hold without permission and pay for it alone. I want a way to predict the payment.”

Tiffani’s mouth flooded with copper again. Her hand tremored once at the wrist.

She made her fingers still by force.

“Mutual utility,” Tiffani said.

Salee nodded. “Terms. You don’t document faces here. You document patterns. You don’t take names unless they offer. You don’t hand my routing to any authority—Guild, Crown, or your own conscience.”

“And you give me access,” Tiffani said.

Salee leaned in. “I give you truth as my people see it. It comes dirty. It comes with holes. You can try to fill the holes with your method, but don’t pretend your slate is cleaner than our hands.”

Tiffani didn’t argue. Arguing was waste.

“Accepted,” she said.

Salee snapped her fingers and a runner stepped in, breathless.

“Saint Merro’s sending more,” the runner said. “Water took the kiln quarter.”

Bram went pale.

Tiffani’s throat tightened and she didn’t let it show. The Brinehammer wasn’t finished costing them. It was still writing its own ledger.

Salee looked at Tiffani as if to say: your institution can’t stop water.

Tiffani opened her slate.

“Then we document faster,” she said, because she didn’t have a better weapon.

---

# CHAPTER 7: TRACKING  
*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6*

The coast smelled wrong.

Not just salt. This was brine mixed with smashed weed and rot lifted from places it didn’t belong and dropped on the surface.

Jhace walked the road north with his kit tight against his ribs. The phase lag made every step slightly mistimed. He adjusted by counting. Beat between command and motion. Beat between landing and correction.

He tracked Mobel by wake, not by sightings.

A dock piling braced too cleanly. A warehouse wall stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at the load joints instead of dispersed across the surface. A stone stair that should have shed its top three steps now holding intact with a copper-gold seam line drawn by a steady hand.

Mobel’s signature.

Now a second factor sat on everything: saturation.

Water undermined mortar, swelled timbers, shifted foundations. It created failures everywhere at once, and the shard inside Jhace responded to each one.

Pressure behind his sternum tightened whenever a stress point was near. Directional. Insistent.

The shard wanted him to fix it.

Every failure point was an invitation.

Jhace refused three invitations before noon and paid for it anyway. Heat built behind his ribs with each refusal. The phase lag worsened. His hands stayed steady, but his body felt less his.

By midday he reached a coastal settlement that had been slapped by the Brinehammer’s edge. Not flooded deep—just enough to break glass, tear doors, soak foundations, and leave people standing around with the look that meant the world had revised itself without asking.

A pier crane leaned at an angle that would kill someone if it fell.

A man in a soaked apron stood under it, staring up like staring could become a brace.

Jhace felt the stress in the crane’s joint as if it were inside his own shoulder.

He walked past.

He made it ten steps before the shard surged without permission.

Light pushed from his sternum into the stone underfoot. It didn’t flare; it invaded. The crane’s joint locked. The lean stopped increasing. The structure accepted a new load path.

The man looked at Jhace like he’d just watched a tool speak.

Jhace broke coupling and kept walking.

Heat slammed through him hard enough that his vision narrowed. Copper flooded his mouth. His stomach rolled. He wiped his mouth with the back of his wrist and saw a thin smear of blood.

His palm glowed brighter. The copper-gold threads had climbed another finger’s width toward his wrist since morning.

He wrote on his slate without stopping:

*A5-T6. Compulsion breached control. Involuntary hold triggered by proximity + saturation stress. Cost: heat spike + oral bleed + spread accelerated.*

He didn’t look back at the crane.

Mobel had been through here recently—three Tides ago, maybe two. Witnesses spoke about “the calm one” who fixed a pier brace and didn’t shake afterward. They spoke about residue left behind that didn’t smear when touched. They spoke about it with gratitude and fear mixed together.

Jhace didn’t ask too many questions. Questions made faces memorable. Faces made him trackable.

He moved north.

Late afternoon brought another consequence of the Brinehammer: displaced people moving inland, dragging carts, hauling soaked bundles, looking back over shoulders as if the sea might follow them up the road.

One woman saw Jhace’s glowing palm when his sleeve shifted.

Her eyes widened. She backed away from him like he was an exposed edge.

Jhace pulled his sleeve down and kept walking.

He wasn’t a savior.

He wasn’t a villain.

He was a problem that moved.

As the sun fell, the road ahead rose toward higher ground. Mobel’s wake led that way—fresh residue at a stone culvert, clean joint locks on a bridge support undermined by floodwater.

Jhace followed.

His chest pressure didn’t ease.

If anything, it felt pleased.

---

# CHAPTER 8: ENTROPY  
*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T7*

Velocity-Heights sat above the Dregs on newer stone and older arrogance.

The streets were cleaner. The buildings stood straighter. The people walked as if collapse was something that happened to other districts.

Corlexi came alone, cover in place: merchant verification, shipment analysis, relief-routing assessment.

Real purpose: Eries.

The Heights had taken Brinehammer pressure in a way its residents could stomach: not water in the streets, but bodies at the gates. Refugees were being funneled into glass-roofed halls and “temporary” shelters built from lattice and pane. The architecture looked precise. The loads were not.

He found Eries in a public garden where glass lattice caught sunlight and broke it into hard lines across the stone path. Students sat around her with open slates, hungry for certainty delivered in calm voice.

Eries spoke without raising volume.

“The Brinehammer isn’t a moral event,” she said. “It isn’t punishment. It isn’t a lesson. It is force meeting structure. If your structure lies to itself, force will correct the lie.”

When the students dispersed, Eries didn’t turn to Corlexi immediately. She watched the last one leave, then spoke as if continuing a thought.

“You’re not here for shipping.”

Corlexi didn’t answer.

“Crown,” she said anyway.

He held his face steady. “You write about decay.”

“I write about reality.” Eries looked at him. “Saint Merro built walls and wrote ledgers. The sea came in anyway. People want someone to blame because blame feels like control.”

“The registry prevents uncontrolled risk,” Corlexi said.

“The registry categorizes it,” Eries replied. “That isn’t prevention. It’s labeling after ignition.”

A shout rose from beyond the garden wall. Not a protest. A warning.

They both turned to see a relief crew dragging glass panes toward a shelter hall. The panes were strapped wrong. The load shifted. A man stumbled. The top sheet flexed and began to bow.

Corlexi saw the stress line forming before it formed. The glass wasn’t annealed for this kind of transport. It was meant to sit, not be hauled through a crowd.

A crack sang out—thin, sharp—and the sheet began to split.

People scattered. Some didn’t move fast enough.

Eries stepped off the path and into the churn with the calm of someone who had accepted that the world breaks whether you agree or not. “Down,” she ordered, and the nearest students dropped without asking why.

Corlexi could have corrected the strap tension with Form and no one would notice what he’d done—only that the glass hadn’t cut anyone.

But the crowd was full of eyes. And above the shelter hall door stood Crown relief guards, crisp, watching.

He moved anyway.

Not with light.

Not with spectacle.

He stepped behind the pane-haulers and put his hands on the wooden brace as if he was steadying it like any other man. He let his focus slip into the strap path and the brace angle and the load distribution.

Form.

A slight correction. A load shift. The strain moved out of the crack line and into the brace. The glass stopped singing. The split halted, held on a hairline.

The haulers froze, then kept moving, because the only way to survive broken glass was to keep it pointed away from flesh.

A guard looked directly at Corlexi.

Eries looked directly at him too, and her gaze was not surprised. It was confirming.

When the immediate crisis passed, Eries returned to the garden path and wiped glass dust from her palm as if dust was the only cost she’d paid.

“You intervened,” she said.

“I prevented waste,” Corlexi replied.

“You prevented blood,” Eries corrected. “And you did it without visible cost markers.”

Corlexi kept his voice flat. “Observation is a habit of yours.”

“Reality is,” Eries said. “So—your registry. Your lists. Your termination thresholds. Do you understand what you’re building.”

“Order,” Corlexi said.

Eries shook her head once. “A story. One you tell while decay continues.”

“Order prevents chaos.”

“Order chooses where chaos is allowed to land,” Eries replied. “Your registry will not stop holds from failing. It will decide who gets blamed when they do.”

Corlexi felt the embedded fragments under his skin pulse faintly, as if they disliked being discussed.

He forced the conversation back into usefulness. “What’s the alternative.”

Eries looked toward the city below, where the Dregs sprawled in patched stone and salvaged beams.

“The alternative is admitting you don’t control failure,” she said. “You manage it. You choose where it lands. And if you pretend you aren’t choosing, you’ll still choose—just blindly.”

Corlexi left the Heights with his cover intact and an additional problem: Eries now had proof that he could correct structure without paying visible cost markers.

That knowledge would spread if she chose to speak.

He couldn’t control that.

He could only control what he did next.

---

# CHAPTER 9: COLD  
*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T8*

The Dregs didn’t keep trails the way stone kept scuffs.

A person could cross a street here and vanish into a stairwell that had three exits, a rooftop bridge, and a back door that opened into someone else’s kitchen. Witnesses didn’t “report.” They traded. They warned. They stayed silent when silence kept them alive.

Salee’s network gave Tiffani everything it could without betraying itself.

Three sightings in two Tides. A man moving wrong—step landing late, body correcting after the fact. Hands steady when they shouldn’t be. A copper-gold glow glimpsed at a sleeve edge. Always northeast. Always moving. Always refusing to stay in one place long enough to become a rumor with a name.

Tiffani mapped each point and watched the geometry degrade.

Not because the pattern was wrong.

Because the pattern stopped being physical.

It became behavioral.

At the Dregs edge, the last reliable sighting dissolved into people saying “maybe” and meaning “don’t ask me again.”

Bram stood beside her under a bent awning and watched her slate.

“You’re doing the thing,” he said.

“What thing.”

“The thing where you pretend your slate is the only real thing in the world.” His voice wasn’t joking now. The Brinehammer had stolen most of his jokes. “He’s not here, Tiff.”

“The data doesn’t support that conclusion.”

“The data doesn’t support any conclusion,” Bram snapped, then caught himself. “Sorry. It’s just—look around. People lie here the way they breathe. Not because they’re evil. Because it keeps them from getting cut open by somebody with a registry.”

Copper rose behind Tiffani’s tongue. She swallowed it down. Her knee twinged hard enough to make her shift weight.

She didn’t write any of that down.

Salee approached without announcement.

“You’ve reached the end of what physics can prove,” Salee said.

“Physics doesn’t end,” Tiffani replied.

“Your access ends,” Salee corrected. “Your assumptions end. Your method has limits here.”

Tiffani kept her chalk steady. “He chose concealment.”

Salee’s eyes narrowed. “Or he chose not to be your experiment.”

That landed harder than it should have. Tiffani didn’t let her face change.

Bram watched her anyway.

“If he doesn’t want to be found,” Bram said, quieter, “then you’re not tracking a body. You’re tracking a decision.”

Tiffani wrote on her slate:

*Trail cold. Physical markers absent. Variable introduced: will.*

She stared at the line until it felt like admitting defeat.

Then she drew another.

*Redirect objective: Mobel.*

Salee nodded once. “That’s smarter.”

“Not smarter,” Tiffani said. “More measurable. Mobel leaves structural outcomes. Outcomes can be mapped.”

Bram’s eyes went to her hands. “And you? Still not logging anything.”

Tiffani’s chalk snapped in her fingers. A clean break. She stared at the broken tip.

“I’m fine,” she said.

Bram didn’t accept it. “Your knee locks. You’re swallowing copper every third sentence. Your hand shakes when you think nobody sees. I see.”

Tiffani put the broken chalk into her kit. “If I log it, I lose access.”

“You’re already losing access,” Bram said. “You’re just losing yourself with it.”

Salee watched them both like she was routing a crisis.

“The Brinehammer pushed refugees this way,” Salee said. “Saint Merro’s failures are leaking into my district. If you want Mobel, you go where stress concentrates. The coast. The broken routes. The places people can’t afford to abandon.”

Tiffani nodded once.

That night, alone, she opened her personal margin and wrote one sentence:

*My body is becoming data I refuse to record.*

Her hand shook while she wrote it.

She still didn’t move it into the official log.

---

# CHAPTER 10: INTERSECTION  
*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T9*

The settlement didn’t have a name anybody agreed on.

It had a lighthouse stump—stone base snapped clean, the tower gone. It had a main street that sloped toward the sea and a line of broken glass along every curb from where the Brinehammer’s edge had slapped through.

Jhace arrived at midday and felt the stress before he saw it.

A warehouse frame pulled out of square. Wet load in the roof. One support joint already eating itself under shifting weight. If it failed, it would fold inward. People were inside hauling salvage.

The shard behind his sternum tightened.

He told it no.

He walked past.

It pulled harder.

He made it to the far side of the street before his body stopped listening to refusal.

Light pressed outward through him and into the stone. The warehouse frame settled. The joint locked. The failure progression slowed enough for the people inside to get out.

Jhace broke coupling and staggered one step as the phase lag hit like a delayed punch.

Heat slammed up his ribs. Copper flooded his mouth. His stomach rolled. He wiped his mouth with the back of his wrist and saw a thin smear of blood.

He kept moving.

He didn’t want witnesses.

“You’re the other one.”

The voice came from behind him, steady, not curious. Not afraid.

Jhace turned.

A woman in practical clothes stood in the lee of a broken doorway. Mid-thirties, maybe. No tremor. No visible Stage markers. A tool hung from her belt—glass and wire and a shard sliver set into the housing so it caught light wrong and threw it off-axis.

It hummed faintly when she shifted her weight.

“Define ‘other,’” Jhace said.

“Internal integration,” she answered. “Like Mobel.”

Jhace’s fingers tightened. “You know him.”

“I know what he leaves.” She stepped closer, just enough to make it clear she wasn’t trying to sneak. “Name’s Vael. I repair what can be repaired. I watch what can’t.”

“You’re watching me.”

“I’m measuring your wake.” Vael’s eyes flicked to his sleeve, then away. She didn’t stare at the glow. She treated it like a fact. “You’re leaving clean locks. Concentrated residue. No visible tremor. And you’re doing it without meaning to half the time.”

Jhace didn’t deny it.

“I need answers,” he said. “Mobel’s the only other one I’ve seen. He’ll know what this is.”

Vael’s mouth tightened. “Mobel’s been running for three Anneals. Whatever he knows, he doesn’t share it anymore.”

“Why not.”

“Because being a solution makes you a target.”

Heat pressed harder behind Jhace’s sternum at that word.

Vael touched the tool on her belt. The hum sharpened. “This is external coupling,” she said. “I built a way to keep the shard out of my meat. It still costs. It just doesn’t rewrite me from the inside.”

“What does it cost,” Jhace asked.

Vael’s eyes stayed on him. “Time. Attention. And the fact I can never put it down. One slip, one missed stress read, and it takes my hand off.”

Jhace looked back down the street where the warehouse now held, temporarily. People were hauling wet timber out. Someone pointed in his direction and then looked away too fast.

“I’m being hunted,” Jhace said.

Vael nodded once. “Yes.”

“You don’t know who.”

“No,” she said. “And you’re going to keep it that way. Because the moment you name a suspect, you’ll start leaving proof for them to use.”

“How close.”

“Two Tides behind you,” Vael said. “Maybe less now. I’ve seen their corrections.”

“Corrections.”

“They don’t just follow,” Vael said. “They tidy. They overwrite residue. They ask questions after you leave and make people forget they answered.”

Jhace’s throat went dry.

Vael leaned in slightly. “Your wake is loud. The Brinehammer broke half the coast; stress points are everywhere. You’re going to keep locking structures because your shard is addicted to failure. That makes you trackable.”

Jhace hated how accurate that was.

“What do you want,” he asked.

Vael’s eyes flicked toward the inland road. “I want you to decide whether catching Mobel is worth being caught yourself. Because if you keep doing this—locking holds in public—you’re going to get intercepted.”

Jhace wrote on his slate, quick:

*A5-T9. Vael confirmed pursuit. Pursuer performs residue correction + witness management. External shard-tool exists; costs differ, not absent.*

He looked up. Vael was already stepping back toward the doorway shadow.

“Why tell me,” he said.

Vael paused. “Because I’ve seen what happens when someone becomes a walking fix. People stop seeing you as a person. They start seeing you as infrastructure.”

She disappeared into the settlement without another word.

Jhace stood alone with his mouth full of copper and his chest full of pressure and the road ahead leading toward Mobel.

He chose forward anyway.

Because answers were a kind of tool.

And he needed one before the cost finished reshaping him.

---

# CHAPTER 11: COERCION  
*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T10*

Corlexi returned to his workspace and knew immediately it had been read.

Not stolen from. Not ransacked. Read.

Dust patterns were wrong. The edge of his merchant ledger sat two finger-widths off where he always aligned it. One pin on the incident map had been rotated slightly, as if someone’s thumb had nudged it while thinking.

Corlexi didn’t move deeper into the room. He stood in the doorway and let his senses do what the Crown paid them for.

Air displacement. Floorboard memory. The faint after-vibration of someone who’d moved carefully.

He stepped in and went straight to the bed corner—because fast searches miss the obvious hiding places and hit the personal ones.

Under his pillow, he found a single copper-stained chalk piece.

Not his.

A maker’s notch cut into the side.

Vael.

A message without ink: *I can reach you where you sleep.*

He didn’t crush it. Crushing it would be emotion. Emotion left patterns.

He tucked it into his palm and left the room by the back stairs.

Outside, the Dregs had changed again. Refugees from Saint Merro had started arriving in steady streams—salt-cracked skin, damp bundles, eyes that kept scanning upward for more water. The Brinehammer’s cost wasn’t just structural. It was behavioral. People flinched at loud sounds now. They packed their valuables higher. They stopped trusting doors.

Salee’s people were routing them. Feeding them. Assigning corners. Keeping panic from becoming riot.

The Crown relief wagon had returned too, and this time the table was bigger.

Two clerks wrote faster. A guard stood closer. A barrel of soup sat behind the pages like bait.

“Name and district,” the clerk called. “Manifestation status if known.”

A man stepped forward, starving, hands shaking. He gave a name and got a bowl.

Then the guard asked the second question quietly, right at the man’s ear: “Who else manifested during the inundation.”

That wasn’t relief.

That was harvesting.

Corlexi watched the exchange and felt the Crown’s directive tighten around his throat. Liaison work. Classification categories. “Non-consensual stabilization.” They weren’t building a safety system. They were building a net.

Vael stood near a broken fountain edge, watching the flow the way a structural inspector watched load distribution.

Corlexi approached and kept his hands visible.

“You searched my workspace,” he said.

Vael didn’t deny it. “I confirmed a suspicion. Crown embedded. Registry architect.”

“Accurate,” Corlexi said. “And you are an independent operator with external coupling technology.”

“Accurate,” Vael replied.

They held each other’s gaze while the Dregs moved around them.

“You could expose me,” Corlexi said.

“I could,” Vael agreed. “Exposure isn’t my goal.”

“Then state your goal.”

Vael’s eyes cut toward the relief table. “Your registry is going to use the Brinehammer as justification. ‘Public safety.’ ‘Stabilization.’ You’ll collect names in exchange for soup.”

Corlexi’s face stayed calm. “Documentation enables resource allocation.”

“Documentation enables control,” Vael shot back. “And control chooses who gets protected. You’ve already started choosing.”

Corlexi didn’t flinch. “Choosing is governance.”

“Choosing is violence,” Vael said, and it wasn’t philosophy—it was operational truth in a district where choices got people killed.

A shout rose near the relief table. A woman refused to give a name. A guard stepped forward. Another guard shifted his baton from one hand to the other.

Salee’s network moved.

Two men carrying boards “accidentally” blocked the guard’s path. A child tripped in front of the table. Soup spilled. The clerks shouted. The guard’s attention snapped to cleanup.

The woman vanished into the crowd.

The registry continued, but it had lost a piece.

Salee passed nearby and didn’t look at either of them, which meant she was absolutely listening.

Corlexi understood the trap: argue here and reveal something; move now and look guilty; refuse and look controlled.

He chose the only move that kept his cover intact.

“I will continue my work,” he said. “Without records, people die in darkness.”

Vael’s mouth tightened. “People die with records too.”

Corlexi placed the copper-stained chalk on the fountain edge between them. “Then give me data that matters. Locations. Signatures. Timings.”

Vael stared at the chalk. “You’re bargaining.”

“I’m always bargaining,” Corlexi said. “That’s what embedded means.”

Vael pocketed the chalk.

“Fine,” she said. “One thing. The pursuer behind the integrated one—Jhace. They don’t move like Crown. Their questions aren’t official. Their corrections aren’t bureaucratic. They are precise.”

Corlexi felt cold settle behind his ribs that had nothing to do with weather.

Precise actors were harder to classify. Harder to control. Harder to predict.

A new sound cut through the square: wood snapping. A shelter wall giving. Too much wet weight, too many bodies pressed against a brace that had been salvaged from a rotten pier.

People shouted. The brace bowed.

Two manifestors reached for it at the same time, filaments flaring. Their coupling overlapped, fought, stuttered. One screamed and fell back, clutching his forearm. The other held for five heartbeats, then dropped, legs giving out.

The wall started to go.

Corlexi’s reflex screamed at him: fix it. Form it. Hold it.

Vael’s eyes flicked to him, sharp as a blade edge. She knew he could.

Salee’s people were in the crush. Refugees were in the crush. A child was in the crush.

Corlexi moved.

He stepped into the chaos with a plank in his hands, as if he was just another man bracing wood. He wedged the plank under the failing joint and pushed with his shoulder.

Then, under that physical exertion, he let Form slide into the brace’s stress line.

Not a miracle. Not a perfect correction. Just enough.

The brace stopped bowing. The wall’s vibration steadied. The load bought time for hands to stack another support and lash it tight.

No visible light. No copper filament. No residue geometry.

But the shift was too clean.

The nearby manifestor—still shaking—looked up at Corlexi with suspicion and relief mixed together. Vael watched him without blinking. Salee’s gaze came from somewhere in the crowd, unseen and present.

Corlexi backed out as soon as the secondary brace took weight. He didn’t wait for thanks. Thanks was attention.

He returned to his workspace and filed one new entry under grain invoices:

*A5-T10. Vael confirmed: unknown pursuer active. Capable of residue correction + witness shaping. Threat classification: non-institutional, precise. Relief registry escalating under disaster cover. Cover strain increased via emergency intervention.*

He stared at the line after he wrote it.

Because for the first time since the Piercing, the enemy in his notes didn’t have a stamp.

---

# CHAPTER 12: LIGHT CRACKS  
*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T11*

The inland road rose into harder ground.

Less brine. Less rot. More stone that hadn’t been soaked and hollowed.

It should have felt safer.

It didn’t.

The shard behind Jhace’s sternum didn’t care about safety. It cared about failure points, and failure didn’t need seawater to exist. It just needed load.

Mobel’s wake was fresh—one Tide ahead, maybe less. A culvert brace locked too clean. A bridge support joint sealed with copper-gold geometry that didn’t match normal dispersal. A barn frame corrected mid-sag with no witness reporting tremor afterward.

Jhace could feel the direction the way he could feel heat through gloves—dull at distance, sharp when close.

He pushed.

The phase lag was worse now. Each step landed late. Each correction arrived after the mistake. He adjusted by slowing, by counting, by refusing to rush even when his body wanted to lunge forward like catching Mobel would stop the pressure in his chest.

It wouldn’t.

The road crossed a bridge where floodwater had scoured the base of the supports. Not a Brinehammer hit this far inland—just the long reach of water that didn’t stay where it belonged.

The bridge groaned under his boots.

Jhace felt the stress in the support joint before the sound finished echoing.

He kept walking.

The shard surged anyway.

Light pressed out from his sternum into the stone and iron. The joint locked. The groan stopped. The bridge held.

Heat slammed through him.

Not just heat. A sensation of tissue shifting under his skin, as if something inside him had decided to continue its work.

He broke coupling and dropped to one knee on the bridge deck.

His mouth filled with copper. He swallowed and tasted blood again, thicker this time.

He looked at his arm.

The copper-gold threads had climbed past his wrist. They sat under the skin in a lattice that didn’t match veins and didn’t match scars. When his heart beat, the glow answered.

He pulled his sleeve higher and saw the edge of the spread near his forearm—new territory, new line.

He wrote on his slate with a hand that stayed steady even as his body shook internally:

*A5-T11. Involuntary hold. Spread advanced beyond wrist. Heat spike severe. Oral bleed recurring. Phase lag worsening. Termination threshold unknown.*

He forced himself upright.

The road behind him was empty.

He didn’t trust that.

Vael’s warning lived in him now: they tidy; they overwrite; they shape witnesses.

He moved off the bridge and into a stand of wind-stunted trees where the road bent and sightlines shortened. The shard pressure eased slightly without an active stress point in range, but the core heat didn’t.

A sound behind him—stone against stone, faint, careful.

Jhace stopped walking and listened.

Nothing.

Then, from farther back, a different sound: a cough suppressed badly. Human. Close enough to mean danger, far enough to mean patience.

The pursuer wasn’t a rumor anymore.

They were distance.

Jhace didn’t turn. Turning was for fights you could win.

He kept moving because Mobel was still ahead, still leaving that clean wake, still holding answers Jhace didn’t have.

Three-way chase, then:

Jhace pursuing Mobel.  
The pursuer pursuing Jhace.  
And the shard inside Jhace pursuing failure points whether he consented or not.

The Brinehammer had rewritten Saint Merro with water and force.

Whatever had rewritten Jhace was doing the same thing to his body—quiet, relentless, structural.

Light cracked through him with every step. Not a flare. Not a spectacle. A steady invasion.

He didn’t know what the final cost would look like.

He kept walking anyway.

Because some questions were worth paying for.

---
