Tools left: primary slate.* Her hand stayed steady. She kept her eyes on the slate while she wrote. Looking at absence invited speculation. Speculation wasn’t data. Footsteps halted in the corridor. Not careful. Not loud. The latch clicked. A medical clerk edged into the doorway—young, trying to look like his spine had been issued with the uniform. “The director wants to see you. About records.” “After.” Tiffani didn’t turn. “Explain the patient evacuation process.” “There wasn’t one.” His mouth tightened at one corner. “No evacuation authorization filed. No transfer order. No discharge.” Tiffani’s chalk paused. “Then he’s still documented as admitted.” “That’s the problem.” The clerk swallowed. Once, then again. “Someone removed the admission ledger page. The director is… unclear what protocol is, now.” Tiffani wrote, smaller, where it wouldn’t be the first thing read if her slate was seized: *Institutional obstruction: logged.* The clerk left fast, as if speed could become innocence. She waited six heartbeats—enough time for panic to get down the hall and start infecting other people—then went to the guard. He was posted outside the door. Young. Fresh uniform. New fabric still holding its fold. “Patient Jhace Torrins,” she said. “Stage Four. Admitted A4-T19. Paralysis from sternum down. Motor function nonexistent. When did he leave?” The guard snapped straighter. “He didn’t. No one entered. No one left. I’ve been here since second bell.” “The door was watched the entire time.” “Yes, ma’am.” “No breaks. No distractions. No message that required you to look away.” His eyes flicked once—down the corridor, then back. His fingers tightened on the door frame. “I checked the corridor. Standard sweep. Maybe forty heartbeats.” “Forty,” Tiffani said, and wrote it down. While her chalk moved, she watched him the way she watched structures under load. Breath shallow. Pupils wide for a corridor that bright. Knuckles pale. He was either lying. Or he’d seen something he didn’t have language for and had decided silence was safer than truth. She didn’t press. Pressure changed behavior. Changed behavior ruined reads. “Thank you,” she said, and left him with his fear. Back inside, she shut the door and stood in the space where the impossible had happened. The grid reference-hum in this wing carried a second mode. It sat under the normal vibration in the stone—lower, deeper, persistent. She’d noticed it yesterday. She hadn’t written it because noticing wasn’t the same as confirming. Copper rose behind her tongue. Stronger than yesterday. Her fingers twitched once as she lifted the chalk. She did not add that line to the slate. If he moved, there was a vector. If someone moved him, there was evidence. Her job was to make the room confess without asking it politely. She started at the threshold and worked clockwise. Doorframe: no fresh marks. Wall: no shoulder smear, no cloth rub, no grit ground into plaster at carry height. Floor: no drag line. Window: latched, dust on the sill undisturbed. The ash bin by the cold stove held half-burned paper. She knelt and sifted with two fingers. Most of it collapsed into black powder. One piece held long enough to read: *…locked maintenance corridor…requires two hands + walking…impossible…* A report. Someone had written it. Someone had decided it needed to vanish. She slid the scrap into her notes. Footsteps again. Faster this time. Not careful. Bram appeared in the doorway, coat rumpled. His face tried to assemble a joke and quit halfway. “Cool,” he said, staring at the empty cot. “Love walking into rooms where the paralyzed guy quits his job and leaves.” “Not left.” Tiffani stood. Ash dust clung to her fingertips; she wiped it onto her trousers without looking. “Moved. Method unknown.” Bram’s jaw worked. “That is not better.” “It’s accurate.” He dropped onto a crate beside the cot. The wood complained. “Tiff. Yesterday he couldn’t move his fingers. Today he’s gone. That’s not clerical.” “No. It’s authority.” She kept her voice level because anger was loud and the building liked to listen. “Someone with reach removed a ledger page and burned a report.” She lifted Jhace’s primary slate—tested the weight, the worn edge where his thumb always rode. No note. No instruction. Just deliberate placement. A control variable left behind. She set it back exactly where it had been. Bram watched her do it. “So what now?” “Now I follow physics,” Tiffani said. “Residue. Mode surges. Stress anomalies. He didn’t evaporate. He left a trail or someone else did.” “And if he doesn’t want to be found?” She didn’t answer. Want was not a measurable property. Her method didn’t have a column for will. She packed instead. Slate. Chalk. Scrap. Calipers. The small wedge gauge she used when she needed the world to admit angles. Bram stood. “You’re going to get us into something bad.” “Define bad.” “The kind where we disappear and somebody burns our paperwork too.” Tiffani closed the kit. The buckles snapped, clean and final. “Then we document faster than they can destroy.” They left the room together. The guard remained at his post. She didn’t look at him. Seven incidents in three Tides. Seven holds that should have failed. Copper-gold residue where it didn’t belong. A second mode in the hum that followed a pattern. Someone was working without paying the visible cost markers. Someone was leaving proof. Tiffani started down the stairs. Her hand trembled once against the rail. Her right knee caught on the third step, locked, then released. She kept walking. She left both facts off the slate. --- The building took a hit. Not a crack of impact—no sharp report, no ringing. A low, muffled bass punch that arrived through the soles of her boots and climbed her shins into her teeth. The stairwell flexed. Dust sifted from a seam overhead. Lamps swung on their chains, lazy at first, then wider. Bram froze mid-step, one hand lifted like it could steady the air. Tiffani stopped breathing long enough to listen with her whole body. The grid reference-hum stuttered. The second mode swelled hard, then dipped, then settled into something heavier. “Quiet,” Tiffani said, and meant it. They stood while the city decided whether it was done being violent. For four heartbeats the only sound was lamp-chain creak and distant shouting that hadn’t learned the new emergency yet. Then came pressure. Not impact. Weight. A broad shove that made the stairwell door tremble in its frame. The hinges squealed. The seam under the door darkened. Water forced itself through first as a line, then as fingers, then as a palm. Cold. Salt-sharp. Laced with silt. It smelled of sea rot and crushed algae and harbor sludge scraped up from below. Bram backed up. “No. No, no—” The door bucked. The frame split at a crack that had been there before today and had just been given a reason to open. Water punched through in a sheet and hit the stairwell wall hard enough to throw spray across the stone. Glass shattered somewhere above them—one of the narrow corridor windows giving up. The next surge came through that opening and down the stairs in a grinding rush that carried splinters, rope ends, and a plank spinning end-over-end. People screamed on the landing below. The sound cut short as the water found them. Tiffani grabbed Bram by the collar and yanked him up, away from the stairwell mouth, toward higher steps. Her kit slammed her hip with each movement. Cold brine licked at the treads behind them. Saint Merro had been built to keep the sea outside. The sea had revised the plan. The Brinehammer was in the streets now, and the city’s stonework was about to learn what force looked like when it didn’t care about authority, ledgers, or locks. --- # CHAPTER 2: FORM *(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T2* The room smelled of chalk dust and old stone. Corlexi sat at a narrow table that served two lives. One life was grain tallies, transport costs, and the harmless boredom of a merchant. The other was pinned to the walls in colored ink and metal points: incident maps, cost progression curves, termination thresholds, name lists compiled without consent because consent was a luxury the Crown didn’t bother buying. A knock came—wrong timing, wrong rhythm. He moved without haste. Charts slid under a merchant ledger. His shoulders settled into the posture that invited no second look. An older woman stood in the doorway. Textile calluses. Eyes that didn’t waste motion. “Message from Salee,” she said. “She says you’re documenting the Dregs.” “I document inventory,” Corlexi replied. “Documentation keeps business from bleeding.” “Salee says documentation is business.” The woman’s gaze stayed fixed on his face. “She wanted you to know she sees you.” She left before he could waste a response. Corlexi waited until the footfall faded, then unbuttoned his shirt to the waist. The scars cut diagonal across his torso—pale seams where copper-gold had fused into tissue during the Piercing. Twelve fragments, each no larger than a thumbnail. He had counted them enough times that the number lived behind his eyes. He took a breath and tested. No tremor. No exhaustion drift. No vein-lines crawling up his forearms. He focused on the crooked lamp bracket across the room. He read it. Iron. Stress at the joint where the mount met the wall. Load tolerance still adequate, but the fasteners had been giving way by degrees for at least two Anneals. He adjusted the structure. The bracket straightened with a soft click and settled as if it had never been wrong. Form. Structure maintained. No visible filament light. No copper spill. No cost marker. The room stayed quiet except for chain sway. That absence made him valuable. It also made him a problem that could be solved. He buttoned up and pulled the collapse reports back into the open. Seven incidents in three Tides. Saint Merro took most of them—still bleeding from the Piercing, still trying to pretend the grid reference-hum was “normal” again. But the holds were wrong. Too clean. Too complete. Casualty projections that should have been met—and weren’t. Residue concentrated in ways standard dispersal didn’t produce. Someone else was operating outside the visible cost curve. He wrote on his slate, not as speculation but as a working risk: *Pattern suggests internal shard integration may suppress standard cost markers. Requires confirmation. Additional subjects needed.* A faint vibration rolled through the wall. Not ambient drift. Something low and distant, like stone being informed that it might have to move. He didn’t look at the window. Looking was a tell. The hallway outside filled with hurried feet. Not panicked—directed. Directed movement meant a network, and networks meant Salee. A second knock, harder. Corlexi swept the charts under the ledger again and opened the door to find a runner with salt on his cuffs and a textile knot tied to his wrist. “Saint Merro got hit,” the runner said. “Water in the streets. People moving. Refugees coming.” Corlexi let his face stay dull, merchant-appropriate. “Why tell me.” “Because you write things down,” the runner said. “And because writing means planning, and planning means we don’t die stupid.” The runner shoved a folded list into Corlexi’s hand. Not names. Needs. Rope. Boards. Dry cloth. Nails. Bread. Something to brace doors that had stopped being doors. Corlexi looked at the list and felt, for the first time, the registry become useful in the way knives were useful. Not moral. Practical. He put the list under the grain ledger, then asked, “Where’s Salee.” “Routing the first wave,” the runner said. “Also—your building is taking load.” “Load.” “People,” the runner snapped. “Too many. Too fast. Floors aren’t meant for it.” The runner left. Corlexi stood in the doorway and listened to his own building. Stone carried the mass clean. Wood carried it late. The disagreement made small noises he’d ignored for months. Now it mattered. He moved down the stairs and into the lower room where residents were already packing. Not a formal shelter—just a place people knew would still have a roof when the Dregs’ patched structures gave out. A woman with brine-stiff hair shoved a bundle under a table. A man hauled a child up onto a bench. Someone slammed the door and the frame answered with a complaint. Corlexi saw it: the central beam had a hairline split at the join, old damage made worse by the sudden shift in weight. It would hold another hour. Maybe two. Then it would let go and take a corner of the floor with it. If it dropped, people would panic. Panic turned into uncontrolled coupling. Uncontrolled coupling turned into bodies. He could intervene. He could also be seen. Salee’s people were in this room. He recognized the quiet ones: eyes that tracked exits, hands that touched beams without looking like they were checking them. Corlexi moved like a merchant checking inventory. He set down a crate, leaned, and let his focus slide into the split join. Form. Quiet. Surgical. He didn’t “fix” it. He redistributed load just enough to buy time. The split stopped widening. The beam’s vibration steadied. Cost: none visible. Risk: immediate. A man near the wall turned his head sharply, as if he’d heard something that wasn’t a sound. The runner with the textile knot looked at Corlexi’s hands. Corlexi straightened, picked up a sack of grain, and made a show of irritation. “If you’re stacking bodies in here, stack them against the stone wall, not mid-span. You want the floor to keep being a floor.” The instruction sounded like merchant complaint. It was also correct. People shifted where they stood. The load redistributed again, now by human choice. That mattered. It meant his adjustment would be blamed on common sense, not on anomaly. The runner nodded once, almost imperceptible, and moved on. Corlexi returned upstairs and wrote a single line on a merchant sheet: *Saint Merro inundation event initiated. Dregs taking refugee load. Structural failure risk increases. Cover risk increases with emergency intervention.* Then, smaller, where it could hide: *Salee sees me. Now her people may have felt me.* He folded the page under grain totals and resumed the posture of a man who cared only about prices. --- # CHAPTER 3: WAKE *(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T3 through A5-T7* Seven reports. Seven incidents. Three Tides. Tiffani laid them across her workspace and didn’t let herself blink too long. Patterns didn’t reveal themselves to people who looked away. Seam collapse near Junction Twelve boundary. Estimated hold duration: two hundred heartbeats. Zero casualties where her structural projection demanded four minimum. Copper-gold residue concentrated at load junctions instead of fanning out through surrounding material. Foundation stabilization at Merchant Row—same residue behavior. Bridge support failure at the southern canal—again. Each site carried a mode surge in the grid reference-hum that didn’t match ambient drift. Not random noise. A signature. She plotted locations on a city map and drew lines with a tool made for straight truth. The geometry didn’t argue. The vector pointed northeast. Grindheim. A quiet scrape at her door. Not a knock. Someone trying not to be recorded by sound. Corran stepped in and shut it behind him. Mid-level Guild administration. Tired eyes. Restricted stamp on the folder in his hand. The expression of a man walking into a decision he already hated. “Unofficially,” he said, “you did not get this from me.” He set the folder down and didn’t sit. “The director is calling it clerical loss,” Corran continued. “Lost ledger pages. Misfiled incident routing. Your search authorization gets revoked next Tide.” Tiffani stood and went to the archive cabinet on instinct. Pulled the Saint Merro incident drawer. It didn’t open. Not stuck—locked. Fresh metal. New keyway. Corran didn’t look at the lock. He stared at the floor like the floor might offer forgiveness. “They moved fast,” Tiffani said. “They moved before I could stop them,” Corran replied. Tiffani let her fingers rest on the new lock just long enough to feel the cold. Then she stepped back. “Good,” she said. “Now it’s provable.” Corran’s throat worked. “Someone doesn’t want you following this.” Tiffani opened the folder. Eighth incident. Grindheim Dregs. Old textile district. Same residue concentration. Same hold cleanliness. Same missing casualties. Her chalk hovered over her slate. “Why help me?” she asked. Corran’s voice dropped. “Because your ledger page wasn’t ‘lost.’ It was removed. I saw the request in the archive before it got scrubbed. Administrative channel. Crown-level reach.” Institutional obstruction: confirmed. Institutional interference: resourced. Tiffani kept her face still. “What do you want.” “When you understand what happened—when you find out what was done to him—send word outside the official channels.” Corran didn’t offer a method. He didn’t need to. “Don’t send it where they can erase it.” He left without waiting for agreement. That, too, was a data point. Two Tides later she found Bram in the Guild hall with a chipped cup and something dark inside it. “I need access to Grindheim,” she said. “Before the revocation lands. You have textile ties and harbor adjacency. Get me a reason that opens doors.” Bram looked up like his body wanted to make a joke and his brain filed a denial. “I’m not a spy. I’m barely functional.” “You can move where I can’t,” she said. “And people talk when you make them laugh.” He exhaled. “That is… accurate.” “Get me in.” His cup hit the table once, hard enough to rattle the saucer. “When this goes bad, I’m haunting you. I’m going to move your chalk. I’m going to rearrange your kit. I’m going to make your straight lines crooked.” Tiffani didn’t look up from her slate. “Accepted.” She returned to her workspace and finished the map. Eight incidents. Then nine, after another report slid to her through a quiet chain of hands that avoided stamps and signatures. The vector held. Grindheim’s Dregs. A place where buildings leaned because they had to, and information moved through people instead of paperwork. As she drew the final line, copper surged behind her tongue. Her right knee locked mid-motion, released, then refused to cooperate for six heartbeats. She gripped the desk edge until function returned. She did not write either fact down. If she logged it, she lost the work. If she lost the work, he stayed missing. So she didn’t log it. She packed her kit. Slate. Chalk. The burned scrap. The incident reports that existed outside official circulation because someone decided truth didn’t qualify as permitted knowledge. Tomorrow they left for Grindheim. Tomorrow she followed the physics trail into a district where institutions held no power and networks held all of it. Tomorrow she became unauthorized on purpose. At the bottom of the stairs outside her workspace, she paused and wrote one line in a personal margin she never showed anyone: *Jhace chose departure. Authority chose concealment. Physics will decide who’s lying.* Her hand shook while she wrote it. She still left the tremor off the slate. --- # CHAPTER 4: SEARCHING *(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T3* He woke expecting paralysis. His fingers moved. Jhace lay still and ran the test in the only order that ever kept him alive: smallest system first, then expand. Right hand: functional. Delay of three heartbeats between intention and motion. Left hand: functional. Same delay. Toes: responsive, but the signal arrived late, as if the line had been rerouted through bad junctions. Legs: sensation present. Not remembered—present. The tremor was gone. He raised both hands into the thin light and watched them. Steady. No shake in the knuckles. No flutter at the wrist. That should have felt like relief. It didn’t. Something else sat in its place—heat behind his sternum, constant and wrong. Not fever. Not pain. Pressure, like a brace tightened one notch too far and left there. He sat up. The room answered with a soft tilt that corrected itself a heartbeat late. His body was lagging behind his own commands. His limbs moved as if they were accepting delayed instructions. He didn’t fight it. Fighting made mistakes. He looked around. Not the infirmary. A narrow maintenance room with a cold pipe run along one wall and a low steel table bolted to the floor. Dust in the corners. Chalk scuffs on stone. Someone had used this place for work and then stopped. His secondary slate lay on the table. His calibration strap beside it. Three copper-stained chalk pieces laid in a line, tips aligned. Not dumped. Set. His primary slate was missing. He remembered leaving it behind. The heavy one stayed in the room where he’d been officially “admitted,” because the heavy one would become evidence in someone else’s hands. He looked at his palm. Copper-gold light sat under the skin in thin threads that weren’t veins and weren’t ink. When he flexed, it brightened. When he stopped, it dimmed but didn’t disappear. Memory came in broken strips. The cot. Unable to move. Unable to speak. Breathing and watching and waiting for the body to betray him completely. A figure in the doorway. Not shadowed. Not lit. Light fell wrong around it. Edges without behavior. The burn in his palm. The feeling of something being drawn through him—every nerve, every vessel, every joint—surveyed and set. He couldn’t remember a face. He couldn’t remember a voice. He couldn’t remember why. Only the certainty that what happened to him wasn’t a rescue. It was a procedure. A low vibration rolled through the building. Not the normal grid reference-hum. Something heavier, slow enough to make the pipe run above him shiver. Then came the sound: a deep, muffled bass strike, distant but clean through stone. He held his breath. Counted. Listened. Pressure followed. Broad. The table legs squealed against their bolts. Dust dropped in a thin curtain from a seam in the ceiling. He stood too fast. The lag punished him; his legs accepted the command late and he had to catch himself on the pipe. Heat surged behind his sternum. Copper flooded his mouth. He tasted brine in it. He pulled his kit to him and strapped it on by muscle memory. The buckles clicked. The strap bit his shoulder. Good. Real. He wrote one line on the secondary slate: *A5-T3. Mobility restored. Tremor absent. Phase lag present. Core pressure constant.* He tested coupling. He focused on the pipe bracket. The coupling didn’t start in his hands. It started behind his sternum. A tight bloom that pushed outward. Light didn’t spill from his fingers; it bled through his ribs into the metal, into the wall, into the stone underfoot. He felt the stress in the bracket joint without touching it. He felt how close it was to slipping. He pushed. The bracket settled. The vibration in the pipe smoothed. The structure complied. Heat surged hard enough to blur his vision at the edges. Clean lines softened into something less reliable. He broke coupling fast. Cost, then: heat. Latency. Core-origin coupling. Tremor replaced. He wrote: *Coupling origin shifted (sternum/core). Cost markers: heat surge + visual edge loss + phase lag worsens post-hold.* Water hit somewhere above him. A slap of brine against stone. Then the sound of glass giving up—one pane, then another—followed by shouting that cut short too quickly. Jhace’s hand clenched on the chalk until the tip snapped. Mobel surfaced in his mind, sharp and immediate. The other shard-bearer. The one who held without visible cost markers. If anyone had language for integration, it was Mobel. Jhace took what he needed and left by the maintenance corridor the burned report had named—locked, requiring two hands and walking. He had both hands. And he could walk. That was the only impossible part of today that mattered. --- # CHAPTER 5: AUTHORITY *(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T3* News didn’t arrive in the Dregs as proclamations. It arrived as pressure. Corlexi was midway through a termination-threshold recalculation when the building’s vibration changed. The grid reference-hum carried a low shove under its normal band. Stone transmitted it cleanly. Wood transmitted it late. A messenger came ten minutes later. Not a Crown courier. Not a Guild runner. A boy with a textile knot tied to his wrist and salt on his sleeves. “Saint Merro got hit,” the boy said. “With what?” Corlexi asked. The boy’s throat worked. “Sea came into the streets. Doors blew. Windows blew. People dragged out like scrap.” Corlexi set his pencil down and kept his face neutral. “How many?” he asked. The boy shrugged. “Enough.” Corlexi slid a coin across the table. The boy took it and vanished. Disaster did that. It turned “eventual” into “now.” He rose and watched the Dregs redistribute load—bundles moved, boards carried, ropes dragged. Salee’s network was visible in the flow. Then came the other wave. A Crown-marked wagon rolled into the district with a canvas banner and a soup pot strapped to the back. Behind it walked two clerks with clean cuffs. Behind them walked guards with the posture of enforcement dressed as aid. The clerks set a folding table in the street and laid down blank pages like they were laying claim. “Displaced residents,” the lead clerk called. “Relief allocation requires identification. Name and residence district. Manifestation status if known. Injuries noted. Supplies issued.” People watched from doorways. Nobody cheered. Soup in exchange for a registry. Corlexi returned to the table and wrote: *A5-T3. Saint Merro inundation event. Secondary effects: displacement + uncontrolled manifestation spikes + increased pressure for Crown registry implementation.* Outside, a manifestor in line tried to hold a buckling awning while also keeping a child from being shoved underfoot. Tremor in both arms. Stage Two pushing into Three. Her hold broke. The awning frame snapped down and clipped a man’s shoulder. The crowd surged. Two people’s hands lit with copper-gold filaments in instinctive coupling. The light jumped to the supports, then stuttered. Both manifestors recoiled, pain in their faces, but the awning stayed up long enough for bodies to clear. Uncontrolled coupling. Unmeasured cost. Perfect justification for Crown paperwork. A guard stepped toward them. Salee’s network stepped faster. Someone shouted “Brace left!” as if that was all the authority required. Someone else shoved a post into place. The crisis moved away from the clerks without ever becoming “an incident” on their pages. Corlexi received a sealed note before noon. Crown wax. Simple. Heavy. He broke it and read: **Directive:** accelerate compilation. Prioritize high-output manifestors. Prepare classification categories for “anomalous cost suppression” and “non-consensual stabilization events.” Assign liaisons to relief points. Relief as a capture mechanism. He folded the paper under invoices and wrote where no one would look twice: *Acceleration acknowledged. Cover risk increased. Salee aware. Relief liaison will tighten scrutiny.* If Saint Merro’s ledgers were burning, the Crown would make new ones. It would use the Brinehammer as ink and call it safety. --- # CHAPTER 6: DREGS *(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T5* Saint Merro didn’t recover. It redistributed damage. Two Tides after the Brinehammer, the lower streets still smelled of salt rot and smashed kelp. The harbor quarter looked chewed—doors ripped from hinges, glass ground into mortar, boards jammed into cracks where the sea had punched through. The infirmary remained standing. That didn’t mean it was functional. It meant it was occupied. Tiffani walked past triage cots lined in the corridor where Jhace had vanished. People lay wrapped in damp blankets with brine-cracked lips and eyes that refused to close. A manifestor shook uncontrollably while a healer held his shoulders down and told him to stop fighting his own nerves. The records door was still sealed. Fresh wax over old wax. Authority didn’t rebuild. It layered. Bram waited for her at the side exit. “You’re going to pretend this didn’t change everything?” he asked. “It changed the environment,” Tiffani said. “Not the methodology.” Bram followed. “That’s the kind of sentence people say right before they die for a principle.” “Then they die consistent,” Tiffani said. He made a sound that wasn’t humor. “You’re insane.” “I’m correct,” she said. She spread her reports. The Brinehammer added failures. Failures added residue. Residue added data. The clean-hold signature still led northeast. Grindheim. Travel was restricted now. Stabilization protocol. Crown co-mark on Guild authority. Her search permission suspended. Corran handed her a textile shipment escort pass with Bram’s name on it and didn’t ask her to promise anything. Promises were paperwork too. That night they left with a textile wagon crew hauling salvage cloth and salt-stiff rope toward Grindheim. Detours around undermined stone. Temporary bridges held by manifestors whose hands shook too hard to hold their own cups. By the time Grindheim’s Dregs rose into view, Tiffani’s knee had locked and released enough times she’d started counting the intervals. She still didn’t log it. They found Salee by following motion. Inside the unsignposted building, Salee stood over a routing surface—chalk marks, string loops, a glass shard set to throw light sideways. “You’re the methodist,” Salee said. “That’s a label,” Tiffani replied. Salee’s eyes moved over her kit. “Labels tell me where to place you.” “I’m looking for Jhace Torrins,” Tiffani said. “He vanished under institutional suppression. His trail points here.” Salee didn’t feign surprise. “Everything points here when the world breaks. Saint Merro got punched. Refugees will come. Authority will follow them. And you walked in with a kit full of order.” “Order is how people survive,” Tiffani said. “People survive by adapting,” Salee replied. “Order comes after the body count stops moving.” “You want my network,” Salee continued. “Witnesses. Routing. Safe passage. The Dregs doesn’t give that away.” “What do you want,” Tiffani asked. Salee tapped a copper-stained chalk piece on the table. “Your protocols. How you map cost. How you decide who is about to die. My people hold without permission and pay for it alone. I want a way to predict the payment.” “Mutual utility,” Tiffani said. “Terms,” Salee replied. “No faces in your notes. No names unless offered. No routing given to authority.” “And you give me access,” Tiffani said. Salee nodded. “Truth as we see it. It comes dirty.” “Accepted,” Tiffani said. A runner burst in. “Saint Merro’s sending more. Water took the kiln quarter.” Bram went pale. Tiffani’s throat tightened. The Brinehammer wasn’t finished costing them. It was still writing. Tiffani opened her slate. “Then we document faster,” she said, because she didn’t have a better weapon. --- # CHAPTER 7: TRACKING *(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6* The coast smelled wrong—brine, smashed weed, rot lifted from places it didn’t belong. Jhace walked north with his kit tight against his ribs. Phase lag made every step slightly mistimed. He corrected by counting. He tracked Mobel by wake: dock pilings braced too clean, warehouse walls stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at joints, stone stairs corrected without the smear patterns tremoring hands left behind. Now saturation made stress points everywhere, and the shard behind his sternum answered each one. He refused three holds before noon and paid for refusal anyway. Heat built. Phase lag worsened. His hands stayed steady while his body felt less his. At a coastal settlement, a pier crane leaned toward collapse. A soaked man stood under it and stared up. Jhace walked past. Ten steps later the shard surged without permission. Light pressed from his sternum into stone. The crane joint locked. The lean stopped. Jhace cut coupling and kept moving. Heat hit hard. Copper flooded his mouth. A thin smear of blood appeared on his wrist when he wiped. His palm glowed brighter. The copper-gold threads climbed another finger’s width toward his wrist. He wrote while walking: *A5-T6. Involuntary hold triggered by proximity + saturation stress. Cost: heat spike + oral bleed + spread accelerated.* Witnesses spoke about “the calm one” who fixed a pier brace and didn’t shake afterward. Mobel. Three Tides ago, maybe two. Jhace didn’t ask too many questions. Faces made him trackable. Refugees moved inland along the road, carts rattling, bundles soaked. One woman saw the glow under his sleeve and backed away like he was an exposed edge. Jhace pulled his sleeve down and kept walking. Mobel’s wake led toward higher ground—fresh residue at a culvert, clean joint locks on a bridge support undermined by floodwater. Jhace followed. His chest pressure didn’t ease. If anything, it felt pleased. --- # CHAPTER 8: ENTROPY *(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T7* Velocity-Heights sat above the Dregs on newer stone and older arrogance. It took Brinehammer pressure as bodies at the gates, not water in the streets. Refugees were being funneled into glass-roofed halls and “temporary” shelters built from lattice and pane. The architecture looked precise. The loads were not. Corlexi came alone under cover: merchant verification, relief-routing assessment. Real purpose: Eries. He found her in a public garden where glass lattice broke sunlight into hard lines across stone. Students sat around her with open slates. “The Brinehammer isn’t a moral event,” Eries said. “It isn’t punishment. It is force meeting structure. If your structure lies to itself, force will correct the lie.” When the students dispersed, Eries didn’t turn to Corlexi immediately. “You’re not here for shipping,” she said. “Crown?” she added, and didn’t wait for him to answer. “You write about decay,” Corlexi said. “I write about reality,” Eries replied. “People want someone to blame because blame feels like control.” “The registry prevents uncontrolled risk.” “The registry labels risk,” Eries said. “After it’s already moving.” A shout rose beyond the garden wall. A relief crew dragged glass panes toward a shelter hall. The panes were strapped wrong. The load shifted. The top sheet flexed and began to bow. A crack sang out—thin, sharp—and the sheet started to split. People scattered. Some didn’t move fast enough. Eries stepped into the churn and ordered, “Down,” and the nearest students dropped without arguing. Corlexi saw the stress line forming. The glass wasn’t annealed for this transport. It was meant to sit, not be hauled through panic. He could correct it with Form. He also knew Crown guards were watching. He moved anyway. He put his hands on the wooden brace like any other man steadying a load. Under that physical exertion, he let Form slide into strap path and brace angle. A slight correction. A load shift. The strain moved out of the crack line. The glass stopped singing. The split halted on a hairline. A guard stared directly at Corlexi. Eries watched him too, not surprised—confirming. When the crisis passed, Eries returned to the path and wiped glass dust from her palm. “You intervened,” she said. “I prevented waste.” “You prevented blood,” Eries corrected. “Without visible cost markers.” Corlexi held his voice flat. “Observation is a habit of yours.” “Reality is.” Eries looked toward the city below. “Your registry will not stop holds from failing. It will decide who gets blamed when they do.” “What’s the alternative,” Corlexi asked. “Admit you don’t control failure,” Eries said. “You manage it. You choose where it lands. If you pretend you aren’t choosing, you’ll still choose—blindly.” Corlexi left the Heights with his cover intact and a new problem: Eries now had proof of what he could do. Knowledge spread on its own schedule. --- # CHAPTER 9: COLD *(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T8* The Dregs didn’t keep trails the way stone kept scuffs. Salee’s network gave Tiffani what it could: three sightings in two Tides. A man moving wrong—step landing late, body correcting after. Hands steady when they shouldn’t be. A copper-gold glow glimpsed at a sleeve edge. Always northeast. Tiffani mapped each point and watched the geometry degrade. Not because the pattern was wrong. Because the pattern stopped being physical. It became behavioral. At the Dregs edge, reliable sighting dissolved into “maybe” and “don’t ask me again.” Bram stood beside her under a bent awning. “He’s not here, Tiff.” “The data doesn’t support that.” “The data doesn’t support anything,” Bram snapped, then softened. “People lie here to stay alive.” Salee approached without announcement. “You’ve reached the end of what physics can prove,” she said. “Physics doesn’t end.” “Your access ends,” Salee replied. “Your assumptions end.” Tiffani kept her chalk steady. “He chose concealment.” “Or he chose not to be your experiment,” Salee said. Bram’s gaze stayed on Tiffani’s hands. “If he doesn’t want to be found, you’re tracking a decision.” Tiffani wrote: *Trail cold. Physical markers absent. Variable introduced: will.* The line felt like admitting defeat. Then she wrote: *Redirect objective: Mobel.* “That’s smarter,” Salee said. “More measurable,” Tiffani replied. Bram looked tired. “Still not logging your own cost.” Tiffani’s chalk snapped. She stared at the broken tip and put it away. “I’m fine,” she said. “You’re lying,” Bram said. “I see.” Tiffani didn’t answer. That night she wrote one sentence in a private margin: *My body is becoming data I refuse to record.* Her hand shook while she wrote it. She still didn’t move it into the official log. --- # CHAPTER 10: INTERSECTION *(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T9* The settlement had a lighthouse stump and a main street lined with broken glass from the Brinehammer’s edge. Jhace arrived and felt stress before he saw it: a warehouse frame pulled out of square. Wet load in the roof. One support joint already eating itself under shifting weight. The shard tightened. He told it no. It surged anyway. Light pressed outward through him and into stone. The warehouse frame settled. The joint locked long enough for people inside to get out. Jhace cut coupling and staggered one step as phase lag hit. Heat slammed his ribs. Copper flooded his mouth. Blood smeared thin on his wrist when he wiped. He kept moving. “You’re the other one.” Jhace turned. A woman in practical clothes stood in a broken doorway’s lee. No tremor. A tool on her belt—glass, wire, shard sliver—hummed faintly. “Define ‘other,’” Jhace said. “Internal integration. Like Mobel.” She stepped closer. “Name’s Vael. I repair what can be repaired. I watch what can’t.” “You’re watching me.” “I’m measuring your wake,” Vael said. “You’re leaving clean locks half the time without meaning to.” “I need answers. Mobel will know.” “Mobel’s been running for three Anneals. He doesn’t share.” Vael’s mouth tightened. “Because being a solution makes you a target.” “I’m being hunted,” Jhace said. Vael nodded once. “Yes.” “How close.” “Two Tides behind you. Maybe less now.” Vael’s eyes stayed steady. “They tidy. They overwrite residue. They ask questions after you leave and make people forget they answered.” Jhace wrote quickly: *A5-T9. Vael confirmed pursuit. Pursuer performs residue correction + witness management. External shard-tool exists; costs differ, not absent.* “What do you want,” he asked. “I want you to decide whether catching Mobel is worth being caught,” Vael said. “Because your wake is loud, and the Brinehammer made stress points everywhere. You’re trackable.” She stepped back toward shadow. “Why tell me,” Jhace asked. “Because I’ve seen what happens when someone becomes infrastructure,” Vael said. “People stop seeing you as a person.” Then she was gone. Jhace stood with copper in his mouth and pressure in his chest and the road ahead leading to Mobel. He chose forward. --- # CHAPTER 11: COERCION *(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T10* Corlexi returned to his workspace and knew it had been read. Dust patterns wrong. Ledger edge misaligned. One pin rotated slightly. Under his pillow: a copper-stained chalk piece with Vael’s maker notch. A message without ink. He pocketed it and left. The Dregs had changed again. Refugees arrived steady—salt-cracked skin, damp bundles, eyes that scanned upward. The Crown relief table had grown. Two clerks wrote faster. A guard stood closer. “Name and district,” the clerk called. A starving man gave a name and got soup. Then the guard asked quietly, close: “Who else manifested during the inundation.” That wasn’t relief. That was harvesting. Vael stood near a broken fountain, watching flow like a structural inspector. “You searched my workspace,” Corlexi said. “I confirmed a suspicion,” Vael replied. “Crown embedded. Registry architect.” “And you’re an independent operator with external coupling technology.” “Accurate.” “You could expose me.” “I could,” Vael said. “Exposure isn’t my goal.” “State your goal.”
