## CHAPTER 7: TRACKING

*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6*

The coast smelled wrong—brine, rot, and silt that belonged under stone, not on top of it.

Jhace walked north with his kit tight against his ribs. Phase lag made every step arrive late. He corrected by counting and refusing to rush.

Mobel’s wake stayed readable: dock pilings braced too clean, warehouse walls stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at joints, stone stairs corrected without the scuff patterns tremoring hands left behind.

The Brinehammer made stress points everywhere.

That made the shard behind his sternum restless.

He refused three holds before noon and paid for refusal anyway. Heat built. Phase lag worsened. His hands stayed steady while the rest of him felt less aligned.

At a coastal settlement, a pier crane leaned toward collapse. A soaked man stood under it and stared up without moving.

Jhace walked past.

Ten steps later the shard surged without permission.

Light pressed out through his ribs into stone. The crane joint locked. The lean stopped.

Jhace cut coupling immediately and kept moving.

Heat punched his chest. Copper flooded his mouth. When he wiped his wrist, a thin smear of blood came away. Not a wound—internal spill.

His palm glowed brighter. Copper-gold threads climbed another finger’s width toward his wrist.

He wrote while walking:

*A5-T6. Involuntary hold triggered by proximity + saturation stress. Cost: heat spike + oral bleed + spread accelerated.*

Witnesses spoke about “the calm one” who fixed a brace and didn’t shake afterward. Mobel. Two or three Tides ahead, depending on who remembered truth and who remembered what kept them safe.

Refugees moved inland along the road—carts rattling, bundles wet, children carried too high to keep them out of water. One woman saw the glow under Jhace’s sleeve and backed away like he was an exposed edge.

Jhace pulled his sleeve down and kept moving.

Mobel’s wake led toward higher ground—fresh residue at a culvert, clean joint locks on a bridge support undermined by floodwater.

Jhace followed.

The pressure behind his sternum didn’t ease.

If anything, it felt more certain.

---

## CHAPTER 8: ENTROPY

*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T7*

Velocity-Heights sat above the Dregs on newer stone and older arrogance.

It didn’t take the Brinehammer as water in streets. It took it as bodies at gates.

Refugees were being funneled into glass-roofed halls and “temporary” shelters built from lattice and pane. The architecture looked precise. The loads were not. Wet bundles, too many feet, panic weight shifting without rhythm.

Corlexi arrived alone under cover: merchant verification, relief routing assessment.

Real purpose: Eries.

He found her in a public garden where glass lattice cut sunlight into hard lines across stone. Students sat around her with open slates.

“The Brinehammer isn’t a moral event,” Eries said. “It isn’t punishment. It’s force meeting structure. If your structure lies to itself, force corrects the lie.”

When the students dispersed, Eries didn’t turn to Corlexi immediately.

“You’re not here for shipping,” she said.

“Crown,” she added, and didn’t wait for an answer.

“You write about decay,” Corlexi said.

“I write about reality,” Eries replied. “People want blame because blame feels like control.”

“The registry prevents uncontrolled risk.”

“The registry labels risk,” Eries said. “After it’s already moving.”

A shout rose beyond the garden wall. A relief crew dragged glass panes toward a shelter hall. The panes were strapped wrong. The load shifted. The top sheet flexed and began to bow.

A crack snapped through the air and the sheet started to split.

People scattered. Some didn’t move fast enough.

Eries stepped into the churn and said one word—“Down”—and the nearest students dropped without argument.

Corlexi read the stress line instantly. The glass wasn’t annealed for this transport. It was built to sit, not be hauled through panic.

He could correct it with Form.

He also knew Crown guards were watching.

He moved anyway.

Hands on a wooden brace like any other man steadying a load. Under that physical cover, he adjusted strap path and brace angle with Form—small correction, clean redistribution.

The glass stopped singing. The split halted as a hairline.

A guard stared directly at him.

Eries watched too, not surprised—confirming.

When the crisis passed, Eries returned to the path and wiped glass dust from her palm.

“You intervened,” she said.

“I prevented waste.”

“You prevented blood,” Eries corrected. “Without visible cost markers.”

Corlexi kept his voice flat. “Observation is a habit.”

“Reality is,” Eries said. “Your registry won’t stop holds from failing. It will decide who gets blamed when they do.”

[ASSISTANT]
bash -lc sed -n '860,980p' /mnt/data/N02_Light_Cracks_Through_Ch1-12.md

[TOOL]
“What’s the alternative.”

“Admit you don’t control failure,” Eries said. “You manage it. You choose where it lands. If you pretend you aren’t choosing, you still choose—blind.”

Corlexi left the Heights with cover intact and a new problem: Eries now had proof of what he could do.

Knowledge spread on its own schedule.

---

## CHAPTER 9: COLD

*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T8*

The Dregs didn’t keep trails the way stone kept scuffs.

Salee’s network gave Tiffani what it could: three sightings in two Tides. A man moving wrong—step landing late, body correcting after. Hands steady when they shouldn’t be. A copper-gold glow glimpsed at a sleeve edge. Always northeast.

Tiffani mapped each point and watched the geometry degrade.

Not because the pattern was false.

Because the pattern stopped being physical.

It became behavior.

At the Dregs edge, reliable sighting dissolved into “maybe” and “don’t ask again.”

Bram stood beside her under a bent awning. “He’s not here.”

“The data doesn’t support that.”

“The data doesn’t support anything,” Bram snapped, then softened. “People survive by lying here.”

Salee approached without announcing herself. “You’ve reached the end of what physics can prove,” she said.

“Physics doesn’t end.”

“Your access ends,” Salee replied. “Your assumptions end.”

Tiffani kept her chalk steady. “He chose concealment.”

“Or he chose not to be your experiment,” Salee said.

Bram watched Tiffani’s hands. “If he doesn’t want to be found, you’re tracking a decision.”

Tiffani wrote:

*Trail cold. Physical markers absent. Variable introduced: will.*

The line felt like taking damage.

Then she wrote:

*Redirect objective: Mobel.*

Salee nodded once. “That’s measurable.”

Bram’s voice dropped. “You still aren’t logging your own cost.”

Tiffani’s chalk snapped. She stared at the broken tip and put it away.

“I’m fine.”

“You’re lying,” Bram said. “I can see it.”

Tiffani didn’t answer.

That night she wrote one sentence in a private margin:

*My body is becoming data I refuse to record.*

Her hand shook as she wrote it.

She still didn’t move it into the official log.

---

## CHAPTER 10: INTERSECTION

*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T9*

The settlement had a lighthouse stump and a main street lined with broken glass left by the Brinehammer’s edge.

Jhace arrived and felt stress before he saw it: a warehouse frame pulled out of square, wet weight in the roof, one support joint already eating itself under shifting load.

The shard tightened.

He told it no.

It surged anyway.

Light pressed outward through him and into stone. The frame settled. The joint locked long enough for people inside to get out.

Jhace cut coupling and took one step wrong as phase lag hit.

Heat slammed his ribs. Copper flooded his mouth. Blood smeared thin on his wrist when he wiped. He didn’t stop to inspect it. Stopping made you catchable.

“You’re the other one.”

Jhace turned.

A woman in practical clothes stood in a broken doorway’s lee. No tremor. A tool on her belt—glass, wire, shard sliver—hummed faintly.

“Define ‘other,’” Jhace said.

“Internal integration,” she said. “Like Mobel.” She stepped closer. “Vael. I repair what can be repaired. I watch what can’t.”

“You’re watching me.”

“I’m measuring your wake,” Vael said. “You leave clean locks half the time without meaning to.”

“I need answers. Mobel will have them.”

“Mobel’s been running for three Anneals,” Vael said. “He doesn’t share. Being a solution makes you property.”

“I’m being hunted,” Jhace said.

Vael nodded once. “Yes.”

[ASSISTANT]
bash -lc sed -n '980,1105p' /mnt/data/N02_Light_Cracks_Through_Ch1-12.md

[TOOL]
“How close.”

“Two Tides behind. Maybe less now.” Vael’s eyes stayed steady. “They tidy after you. They correct residue. They ask questions after you leave and make people forget they answered.”

Jhace wrote fast:

*A5-T9. Vael confirmed pursuit. Pursuer performs residue correction + witness management. External shard-tool exists; costs differ, not absent.*

“What do you want,” he asked.

“I want you to decide if catching Mobel is worth being caught,” Vael said. “The Brinehammer turned the coast into a field of failure points. Your shard can’t shut up. You’re trackable.”

She stepped back toward shadow.

“Why tell me.”

Vael’s jaw tightened. “Because I’ve seen what happens when someone becomes infrastructure.”

Then she was gone.

Jhace stood with copper in his mouth and pressure in his chest and road ahead leading toward Mobel.

He chose forward.

## CHAPTER 11: COERCION

*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T10*

Corlexi returned to his workspace and knew it had been read.

Dust patterns were wrong. Ledger edge shifted. One pin rotated slightly—small, but deliberate.

Under his pillow: a copper-stained chalk piece with a maker notch he didn’t use.

Vael.

A message without ink: *I can enter your life whenever I want.*

He pocketed it and left.

The Dregs had changed again. Refugees arrived steady—salt-cracked skin, damp bundles, eyes scanning upward for beams that might decide to fail.

The Crown relief table had grown. Two clerks wrote faster. A guard stood closer. Another guard stood behind, watching faces instead of hands.

“Name and district,” the clerk called.

A starving man gave a name and got soup.

The guard leaned in after, voice low and practiced: “Who manifested during the inundation. Who held. Who glowed.”

That wasn’t relief.

That was harvesting.

Corlexi tracked the flow and saw Salee’s counter-flow—quiet diversions, redirected lines, people moved out of the capture funnel without making it obvious. The Dregs didn’t fight head-on. It rerouted.

Vael stood near a broken fountain, watching the street like it was a load diagram. Her shard-tool hummed faintly under cloth.

“You searched my workspace,” Corlexi said when he reached her.

“I confirmed what you are,” Vael replied. “Embedded Crown. Registry architect.”

“And you,” Corlexi said, “are an independent operator with external coupling tech.”

“Accurate.”

“You could expose me.”

“I could.” Vael didn’t smile. “Exposure isn’t my goal.”

“State your goal.”

Vael’s eyes flicked to the relief table. “That table will turn into a net. They’ll call it help. They’ll call your paperwork ‘safety.’ Then they’ll start deciding who gets to live near stable stone and who gets pushed back into rot.”

“You’re describing inevitability,” Corlexi said.

“I’m describing what your Crown does when it panics,” Vael said. “And the Brinehammer made them panic.”

Corlexi kept his posture calm. “So why contact me.”

“Because you’re useful,” Vael said. “You see patterns. You measure costs. You understand thresholds. And your shards don’t make you pay in obvious ways.”

Corlexi’s voice stayed flat. “You want access to my charts.”

“I want leverage,” Vael corrected. “If they come for me, I want proof of what you’ve been building. If they come for the Dregs, I want your termination logic on record. Not in Crown archives. Somewhere that survives.”

“You’re asking me to sabotage my directive.”

“I’m asking you to plan for when your directive gets used as a blade,” Vael said. “Because you’re already writing categories for ‘anomalous cost suppression.’ You’re already drawing a box to put people in.”

Corlexi watched the relief line. A woman with tremoring hands stood three places from the clerk, brine still crusted on her hair. Her child clung to her waist. The clerk’s pen hovered, waiting to turn a human into a row.

“What do you know about Mobel,” Corlexi asked, quietly.

Vael’s gaze sharpened. “Enough to know you shouldn’t say his name in open air.”

Corlexi didn’t react. “And the other anomaly. Clean holds without visible cost.”

Vael’s tool gave a faint hum, like glass under strain. “Cost doesn’t vanish. It changes address. If you haven’t learned where it’s going, you’re building blind.”

Eries’s words echoed without being present: *where does the cost go.*

Corlexi looked back at Vael. “What are you offering.”

“A trade,” Vael said. “You give me copies—select pages, not your whole spine. I keep your cover intact in the Dregs. And I tell you one thing your Crown doesn’t have yet.”

“Which is.”

Vael leaned in just enough to keep her words from traveling. “Someone is cleaning wakes. Not yours. Not mine. Jhace’s. Mobel’s. They move behind the trail and make witnesses uncertain. They scrub residue. They are not Crown clerks.”

Corlexi’s pulse stayed steady by practice. “How do you know.”

“Because I do some of it myself,” Vael said. “And whoever’s behind them is better.”

Corlexi stared at the relief table. The guard leaned in again. Pen moved. A name became a hook.

“Your cover is already strained,” Vael said. “Salee sees you. The Brinehammer gave you excuses to intervene. Every excuse is a footprint.”

Corlexi made a decision he didn’t like.

“Tonight,” he said. “I’ll prepare copies.”

Vael nodded once. “Smart.”

[ASSISTANT]
bash -lc sed -n '1105,1265p' /mnt/data/N02_Light_Cracks_Through_Ch1-12.md

[TOOL]
Vael nodded once. “Smart.”

“And if I refuse.”

“Then the Crown takes your notes anyway,” Vael said. “And you get to pretend you were surprised.”

Corlexi didn’t answer.

He walked away with the chalk piece in his pocket and the ugly certainty that the Brinehammer hadn’t just flooded streets.

It had flooded systems.

And systems, when flooded, showed you exactly where they were cracked.

## CHAPTER 12: PRESSURE

*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T11*

After the Brinehammer, language got sharp.

People stopped saying *damage* when they meant *missing*. Stopped saying *help* when they meant *trade*. Stopped saying *stable* unless they’d just touched the beam and felt it agree.

Salee’s building stayed unmarked from the street. Inside, it had grown teeth—more string lines, more chalk loops, more bodies moving through tight decisions without asking permission from a stamp.

Bram stood at the edge of the routing surface and watched a runner point, then point again, then shove a wedge into a woman’s hand like it was a ration. The woman nodded and moved. No committee. No paperwork. No “please.”

It was brutal.

It worked.

Tiffani adjusted her kit strap and waited until Salee’s eyes came up.

“You’re late,” Salee said.

“I was verifying,” Tiffani replied.

“Verifying what.”

Tiffani put her slate down. “The last three sightings. All of them. Including the ones your runners were told to stop repeating.”

Salee didn’t smile. “You want Mobel.”

“I want the convergence,” Tiffani said. “Mobel is the only known operator who holds clean without the public cost markers. He’s the fastest way to understand what happened to Jhace.”

Bram made a small sound—half warning, half fatigue.

Salee tapped the table twice. A runner stepped forward and laid down a strip of textile with four knots tied into it. No words. Just timing, direction, and the kind of caution you only see after people get punished for talking.

Tiffani read it, transferred it to her map, and felt the vector tighten again.

Northeast. Always northeast.

The trail hadn’t died.

It had learned to be thin.

“Your city is closing,” Salee said. “Not with locks. With movement.”

“They don’t have the bodies to seal everything,” Bram said.

Salee looked at him like he’d offered prayer as engineering. “They don’t need everything. They need chokepoints. They need fear. They need a reason to stop a cart and open a kit.”

Tiffani wrote:

*Constraint shift: movement is now regulated variable. Expect witness degradation. Expect residue suppression.*

Salee watched her chalk. “You’re still trying to turn will into physics.”

“I’m trying to remove it as a factor,” Tiffani said.

“You can’t remove it,” Salee replied. “You can only pretend you did.”

A runner burst in—wet cuffs, brine ground into the fabric.

“Relief table moved two streets closer,” the runner said. “They’re marking doors. And they’ve got a list.”

Salee didn’t blink. “What kind.”

“Names when they can get them. If they can’t, they mark by behavior.” The runner’s eyes flicked to Tiffani’s kit. “They asked for the methodist.”

Bram went still.

Tiffani didn’t.

“How,” she asked.

“Clerk said ‘the one who measures cost.’ Like it was praise.” The runner swallowed. “Guard said your name out loud.”

So: the Crown didn’t just know she existed.

It knew what she did.

Copper rose behind her tongue—hard, metallic, immediate. The second band in the hum felt heavier than it had a Tide ago, as if the stone had started carrying a new kind of memory.

Her fingers twitched once around the chalk.

She wrote anyway.

*Observer compromised. Response: accelerate field confirmation. Reduce paper trail.*

Salee leaned in. “You leave tonight.”

“I can’t,” Tiffani said. “My access—”

“Your access is a story you’re telling yourself,” Salee cut in. “They just proved it. You don’t have a key. You have minutes.”

Tiffani shifted her weight before her knee could betray her again. Small correction. Practiced. Not logged.

Bram watched her and didn’t speak. That was worse than teasing. Teasing was denial. Silence was confirmation.

Salee jerked her chin toward the back room. “Come.”

They moved down a narrow hall lined with braces that had been re-set too many times. Each repair was a confession: this place survived by refusing clean failure.

In the back room, a man sat at a table with his head down. Both hands were wrapped in cloth.

He looked up when they entered.

His eyes were too clear—awake in a way that didn’t soften.

He didn’t stand. He didn’t greet. He watched the room like it was a problem he already solved once and didn’t want to solve again.

Salee said one word. “Mobel.”

Bram forgot to breathe for a heartbeat.

Tiffani kept her face neutral and took the room apart: door placement, distances, load-bearing corners, the way the lamp chain wasn’t moving even though the building’s hum had shifted.

Mobel’s gaze went straight to her slate.

“You’re the one turning people into columns,” he said.

“I’m the one trying to keep them alive,” Tiffani replied.

Mobel’s mouth moved like it wanted to reject the word and couldn’t find a cleaner substitute. “Alive,” he repeated. “And indexed.”

“I don’t work for the Crown,” Tiffani said.

Mobel’s eyes slid to Salee and back. “Everyone works for something.”

Tiffani set her slate down open—no concealment, no margin tricks.

“Jhace Torrins disappeared from an infirmary cot in Saint Merro,” she said. “Ledger page removed. Report burned. Secondary band in the hum. Copper-gold residue concentrating where dispersal says it shouldn’t. He was moved or altered.”

Mobel didn’t flinch.

That alone told her she’d hit truth.

“You know,” Tiffani said.

“I know enough to tell you your measurement is incomplete,” Mobel replied.

“I measure manifestation cost,” she said.

“You measure what shows,” Mobel corrected. “You don’t measure what gets reassigned.”

Tiffani held her chalk still by force. “Reassigned to where.”

Mobel leaned forward. The cloth at his wrists shifted.

[ASSISTANT]
def chapter(n):
    patt = rf"(## CHAPTER {n}:[\s\S]*?)(?=\n## CHAPTER {n+1}:|\Z)"
    m = re.search(patt, text)
    return m.group(1) if m else ""
for n in [7,9,10]:
    ch = chapter(n)
    print("CH", n, "len", len(ch), "wc", len(re.findall(r'\b[\w’\'-]+\b', ch)))
    print(ch[:800])
    print("---\n")

## CHAPTER 7: TRACKING

*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6*

The coast smelled wrong—brine, rot, and silt that belonged under stone, not on top of it.

Jhace walked north with his kit tight against his ribs. Phase lag made every step arrive late. He corrected by counting and refusing to rush.

Mobel’s wake stayed readable: dock pilings braced too clean, warehouse walls stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at joints, stone stairs corrected without the scuff patterns tremoring hands left behind.

The Brinehammer made stress points everywhere.

## CHAPTER 7: TRACKING

*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6*

The coast smelled wrong—brine, rot, and silt that belonged under stone, not on top of it.

Jhace walked north with his kit tight against his ribs. Phase lag made every step arrive late. He corrected by counting and refusing to rush.

Mobel’s wake stayed readable: dock pilings braced too clean, warehouse walls stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at joints, stone stairs corrected without the scuff patterns tremoring hands left behind.

The Brinehammer made stress points everywhere.

That made the shard behind his sternum restless.

He refused three holds before noon and paid for refusal anyway. Heat built. Phase lag worsened. His hands stayed steady while the rest of him felt less aligned.

At a coastal settlement, a pier crane leaned toward collapse. A soaked man stood under it and stared up without moving.

Jhace walked past.

Ten steps later the shard surged without permission.

Light pressed out through his ribs into stone. The crane joint locked. The lean stopped.

Jhace cut coupling immediately and kept moving.

Heat punched his chest. Copper flooded his mouth. When he wiped his wrist, a thin smear of blood came away. Not a wound—internal spill.

His palm glowed brighter. Copper-gold threads climbed another finger’s width toward his wrist.

He wrote while walking:

*A5-T6. Involuntary hold triggered by proximity + saturation stress. Cost: heat spike + oral bleed + spread accelerated.*

Witnesses spoke about “the calm one” who fixed a brace and didn’t shake afterward. Mobel. Two or three Tides ahead, depending on who remembered truth and who remembered what kept them safe.

Refugees moved inland along the road—carts rattling, bundles wet, children carried too high to keep them out of water. One woman saw the glow under Jhace’s sleeve and backed away like he was an exposed edge.

Jhace pulled his sleeve down and kept moving.

Mobel’s wake led toward higher ground—fresh residue at a culvert, clean joint locks on a bridge support undermined by floodwater.

Jhace followed.

The pressure behind his sternum didn’t ease.

If anything, it felt more certain.

---

## CHAPTER 8: ENTROPY

*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T7*

Velocity-Heights sat above the Dregs on newer stone and older arrogance.

It didn’t take the Brinehammer as water in streets. It took it as bodies at gates.

Refugees were being funneled into glass-roofed halls and “temporary” shelters built from lattice and pane. The architecture looked precise. The loads were not. Wet bundles, too many feet, panic weight shifting without rhythm.

Corlexi arrived alone under cover: merchant verification, relief routing assessment.

Real purpose: Eries.

He found her in a public garden where glass lattice cut sunlight into hard lines across stone. Students sat around her with open slates.

“The Brinehammer isn’t a moral event,” Eries said. “It isn’t punishment. It’s force meeting structure. If your structure lies to itself, force corrects the lie.”

When the students dispersed, Eries didn’t turn to Corlexi immediately.

“You’re not here for shipping,” she said.

“Crown,” she added, and didn’t wait for an answer.

“You write about decay,” Corlexi said.

“I write about reality,” Eries replied. “People want blame because blame feels like control.”

*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T9*

The settlement had a lighthouse stump and a main street lined with broken glass left by the Brinehammer’s edge.

Jhace arrived and felt stress before he saw it: a warehouse frame pulled out of square, wet weight in the roof, one support joint already eating itself under shifting load.

The shard tightened.

He told it no.

It surged anyway.

Light pressed outward through him and into stone. The frame settled. The joint locked long enough for people inside to get out.

Jhace cut coupling and took one step wrong as phase lag hit.

Heat slammed his ribs. Copper flooded his mouth. Blood smeared thin on his wrist when he wiped. He didn’t stop to inspect it. Stopping made you catchable.

“You’re the other one.”

Jhace turned.

A woman in practical clothes stood in a broken doorway’s lee. No tremor. A tool on her belt—glass, wire, shard sliver—hummed faintly.

“Define ‘other,’” Jhace said.

“Internal integration,” she said. “Like Mobel.” She stepped closer. “Vael. I repair what can be repaired. I watch what can’t.”

“You’re watching me.”

“I’m measuring your wake,” Vael said. “You leave clean locks half the time without meaning to.”

“I need answers. Mobel will have them.”

“Mobel’s been running for three Anneals,” Vael said. “He doesn’t share. Being a solution makes you property.”

“I’m being hunted,” Jhace said.

Vael nodded once. “Yes.”

“How close.”

“Two Tides behind. Maybe less now.” Vael’s eyes stayed steady. “They tidy after you. They correct residue. They ask questions after you leave and make people forget they answered.”

Jhace wrote fast:

*A5-T9. Vael confirmed pursuit. Pursuer performs residue correction + witness management. External shard-tool exists; costs differ, not absent.*

“What do you want,” he asked.

“I want you to decide if catching Mobel is worth being caught,” Vael said. “The Brinehammer turned the coast into a field of failure points. Your shard can’t shut up. You’re trackable.”

She stepped back toward shadow.

“Why tell me.”

Vael’s jaw tightened. “Because I’ve seen what happens when someone becomes infrastructure.”

Then she was gone.

Jhace stood with copper in his mouth and pressure in his chest and road ahead leading toward Mobel.

He chose forward.

## CHAPTER 11: COERCION

*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T10*

Corlexi returned to his workspace and knew it had been read.

Dust patterns were wrong. Ledger edge shifted. One pin rotated slightly—small, but deliberate.

Under his pillow: a copper-stained chalk piece with a maker notch he didn’t use.

Vael.

A message without ink: *I can enter your life whenever I want.*

He pocketed it and left.

The Dregs had changed again. Refugees arrived steady—salt-cracked skin, damp bundles, eyes scanning upward for beams that might decide to fail.

The Crown relief table had grown. Two clerks wrote faster. A guard stood closer. Another guard stood behind, watching faces instead of hands.

“Name and district,” the clerk called.

A starving man gave a name and got soup.

The guard leaned in after, voice low and practiced: “Who manifested during the inundation. Who held. Who glowed.”

That wasn’t relief.

That was harvesting.

Corlexi tracked the flow and saw Salee’s counter-flow—quiet diversions, redirected lines, people moved out of the capture funnel without making it obvious. The Dregs didn’t fight head-on. It rerouted.

Vael stood near a broken fountain, watching the street like it was a load diagram. Her shard-tool hummed faintly under cloth.

## CHAPTER 9: COLD

*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T8*

The Dregs didn’t keep trails the way stone kept scuffs.

Salee’s network gave Tiffani what it could: three sightings in two Tides. A man moving wrong—step landing late, body correcting after. Hands steady when they shouldn’t be. A copper-gold glow glimpsed at a sleeve edge. Always northeast.

Tiffani mapped each point and watched the geometry degrade.

Not because the pattern was false.

Because the pattern stopped being physical.

It became behavior.

At the Dregs edge, reliable sighting dissolved into “maybe” and “don’t ask again.”

Bram stood beside her under a bent awning. “He’s not here.”

“The data doesn’t support that.”

“The data doesn’t support anything,” Bram snapped, then softened. “People survive by lying here.”

Salee approached without announcing herself. “You’ve reached the end of what physics can prove,” she said.

“Physics doesn’t end.”

“Your access ends,” Salee replied. “Your assumptions end.”

Tiffani kept her chalk steady. “He chose concealment.”

“Or he chose not to be your experiment,” Salee said.

Bram watched Tiffani’s hands. “If he doesn’t want to be found, you’re tracking a decision.”

Tiffani wrote:

*Trail cold. Physical markers absent. Variable introduced: will.*

The line felt like taking damage.

Then she wrote:

*Redirect objective: Mobel.*

Salee nodded once. “That’s measurable.”

Bram’s voice dropped. “You still aren’t logging your own cost.”

Tiffani’s chalk snapped. She stared at the broken tip and put it away.

“I’m fine.”

“You’re lying,” Bram said. “I can see it.”

Tiffani didn’t answer.

That night she wrote one sentence in a private margin:

*My body is becoming data I refuse to record.*

Her hand shook as she wrote it.

She still didn’t move it into the official log.

## CHAPTER 4: SEARCHING

*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T3*

He woke expecting paralysis.

His fingers moved.

Jhace stayed still and ran the test the way he ran every inspection: smallest system first, then expand.

Right hand: functional. Delay of three heartbeats between intention and motion.  
Left hand: functional. Same delay.  
Toes: responsive, but late—as if signals had been rerouted through bad junctions.  
Legs: sensation present. Not memory. Present.

The tremor was gone.

He raised his hands into thin light. Steady. No flutter at the wrist. No shake in the knuckles.

Relief didn’t arrive.

Heat sat behind his sternum instead. Constant pressure, not pain. Too tight to ignore, too steady to call “temporary.”

He sat up.

The room tilted, corrected a heartbeat late. His body lagged behind command. He didn’t fight it. Fighting made mistakes.

Not the infirmary.

A narrow maintenance room. Cold pipe run on one wall. Low steel table bolted to the floor. Dust in corners. Chalk scuffs on stone.

His secondary slate lay on the table. Calibration strap beside it. Three copper-stained chalk pieces set in a line, tips aligned.

Not dumped. Placed.

His primary slate was missing.

He remembered leaving it behind—the heavy one, the one that could become evidence in the wrong hands. He’d left it where “admission” could be altered and blamed.

He looked at his palm.

Copper-gold light sat under the skin in thin threads that weren’t veins and weren’t ink. Flex: brighter. Still: dimmer. Never gone.

Memory came in strips.

The cot. No voice. No movement. Breathing and waiting.

A figure in the doorway.

Not shadowed. Not lit. Light behaved wrong around it.

A burn in his palm.

The sensation of being mapped—nerve, vessel, joint—surveyed and set.

No face. No voice. No reason.

Only certainty: it wasn’t rescue.

It was procedure.

A low vibration rolled through the structure. Heavy enough to make the pipe run above him tremble.

Then the impact—deep, muffled, clean through stone.

Pressure followed. The steel table complained against its bolts. Dust fell from a ceiling seam.

He stood too fast. Lag punished him; his legs accepted the command late and he caught himself on the pipe.

Heat surged behind his sternum. Copper flooded his mouth.

Brine rode in it.

He strapped his kit on by muscle memory. Buckles clicked. Strap bit his shoulder. Good. Real.

He wrote one line on the secondary slate:

*A5-T3. Mobility restored. Tremor absent. Phase lag present. Core pressure constant.*

He tested coupling.

Focused on a pipe bracket.

The coupling didn’t start in his hands.

It started behind his sternum. Tight bloom, outward push. Light bled through ribs into metal and wall. He felt stress in the joint without touching it.

He pushed.

The bracket settled. Pipe vibration smoothed. Structure complied.

Heat hit hard enough to soften his vision at the edges.

He broke coupling immediately.

Cost: heat. Visual edge loss. Phase lag worsening post-hold.

He wrote:

[ASSISTANT]
bash -lc sed -n '560,650p' /mnt/data/N02_Light_Cracks_Through_Ch1-12.md

[TOOL]
*Coupling origin shifted (sternum/core). Cost markers: heat surge + peripheral blur + phase lag worsens after hold.*

Above him, water struck stone. Then glass gave up—one pane, then another—followed by shouting that cut short too quickly.

Mobel surfaced in his mind like a tool finally remembered.

The other shard-bearer. The one who held without visible cost markers.

If anyone had language for integration, it was Mobel.

Jhace took what he needed and left through the maintenance corridor named in a burned report—locked, requiring two hands and walking.

He had both hands.

He could walk.

That was enough.

## CHAPTER 5: AUTHORITY

*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T3*

In the Dregs, news arrived as pressure before it arrived as words.

Corlexi was recalculating a termination curve when the building’s vibration changed—low shove under the normal band. Stone transmitted it clean. Wood transmitted it late.

A boy with a textile knot on his wrist arrived ten minutes after.

“Saint Merro got struck,” the boy said.

“With what,” Corlexi asked.

The boy’s throat worked. “Sea came into streets. Doors blew. Glass blew. People got dragged like salvage.”

Corlexi set his pencil down. Kept his face neutral.

“How many,” he asked.

“Enough,” the boy said, then took a coin and vanished.

Disaster did that. It turned “later” into “now.”

Corlexi watched the Dregs shift. Bundles moved. Boards carried. Rope dragged. Salee’s network was visible in the routing—fast, quiet, efficient.

Then the second wave arrived.

A Crown-marked wagon rolled into the district with a canvas banner and a soup pot strapped to the back. Two clerks with clean cuffs followed. Guards followed them, posture rigid, hands near batons.

The clerks set a folding table in the street and laid down blank pages like they were claiming territory.

“Displaced residents,” the lead clerk called. “Relief allocation requires identification. Name and district. Manifestation status if known. Injuries noted. Supplies issued.”

People watched from doorways. Nobody cheered.

Soup in exchange for a registry.

Corlexi wrote:

*A5-T3. Brinehammer displacement event. Secondary effects: uncontrolled coupling spikes + increased Crown leverage to implement registry via relief.*

A manifestor in line tried to hold a buckling awning while keeping a child from being shoved underfoot. Tremor in both arms. Stage Two pushing into Three. Her hold broke. The frame snapped and clipped a man’s shoulder.

Crowd surged.

Two hands lit with copper-gold filaments—instinct coupling. Light jumped, stuttered, then held long enough for bodies to clear. Both manifestors recoiled, pain in their faces, and one retched brine onto the street.

A guard stepped toward them.

Salee’s network moved faster.

Someone shouted “Brace left!” and a post went into place. The crisis shifted away from the clerks without becoming a neat incident on their pages.

Before noon, Corlexi received a sealed note.

Crown wax. Simple. Heavy.

He read:

**Directive:** accelerate compilation. Prioritize high-output manifestors. Prepare classification categories for anomalous cost suppression and non-consensual stabilization events. Assign liaisons to relief points.

Relief as capture.

He folded it under invoices and wrote where no one would look twice:

*Acceleration acknowledged. Cover risk increased. Salee aware. Relief liaison will tighten scrutiny.*

If Saint Merro’s ledgers were burning, the Crown would draft new ones using brine as ink and call it safety.

## CHAPTER 3: WAKE

*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T3 through A5-T7*

Seven reports. Seven incidents. Three Tides.

Tiffani laid them across her workspace and refused long blinks. Patterns showed themselves when you didn’t flinch.

Seam collapse near Junction Twelve boundary. Estimated hold duration: two hundred heartbeats. Zero casualties where her structural projection demanded four minimum. Copper-gold residue concentrated at load junctions instead of dispersing into surrounding material.

Foundation stabilization at Merchant Row—same residue behavior.

Bridge support failure at the southern canal—again.

Each site carried a mode surge in the grid reference-hum that didn’t match ambient drift. Not noise. A signature.

She plotted locations on a city map and drew lines with calibrated tools. The geometry held.

The vector pointed northeast.

Grindheim.

A quiet scrape at her door. Not a knock. Someone trying not to exist in the corridor’s memory.

Corran stepped in and shut it behind him. Mid-level Guild administration. Tired eyes. Restricted stamp on the folder in his hand. A man walking into a decision he already hated.

“Unofficially,” he said, “you did not get this from me.”

He set the folder down. Didn’t sit.

“The director is calling it clerical loss,” Corran continued. “Lost ledger pages. Misfiled routing. Your search authorization gets revoked next Tide.”

Tiffani moved on instinct to the archive cabinet. Pulled the Saint Merro incident drawer.

It didn’t open.

Not stuck—locked. Fresh metal. New keyway.

Corran stared at the floor.

“They moved fast,” Tiffani said.

“They moved before I could stop them,” Corran replied.

She rested her fingers on the new lock long enough to feel its cold, then stepped back.

“Good,” she said. “Now it’s measurable.”

Corran’s throat worked. “Someone doesn’t want you following this.”

She opened the folder.

Eighth incident. Grindheim Dregs. Old textile district. Same residue concentration. Same hold cleanliness. Same missing casualties.

“Why help me,” she asked.

“Because your ledger page wasn’t lost,” Corran said, low. “It was removed. I saw the request in archive before it got scrubbed. Administrative channel. Crown-level reach.”

Obstruction: confirmed.

Interference: funded.

Tiffani kept her face still. “What do you want.”

“When you understand what happened—when you learn what was done to him—send word outside the official channels.” Corran didn’t offer method. “Don’t send it where they can erase it.”

He left without waiting for agreement.

Two Tides later, she found Bram in the Guild hall with a chipped cup and something dark inside it.

“I need access to Grindheim,” she said. “Before revocation lands. You have textile ties and harbor adjacency. Get me a reason that opens doors.”

Bram looked up like his mouth wanted humor and his eyes refused it. “I’m not a spy.”

“You can move where I can’t,” she said. “And people talk when they don’t feel threatened.”

He exhaled once. “That is… unfortunately true.”

“Get me in.”

His cup hit the table hard enough to rattle the saucer. “When this goes bad, I’m haunting you. I’ll move your chalk. I’ll swap your measurements. I’ll make your lines crooked.”

Tiffani didn’t look up. “Accepted.”

She returned to her workspace and finished the map.

Eight incidents. Then nine, after another report arrived through hands that avoided stamps and signatures. The vector held: Grindheim’s Dregs. A place where buildings leaned because standing alone was expensive, and information moved through people instead of paperwork.

As she drew the final line, copper surged behind her tongue.

Her right knee locked mid-motion, released, then refused to cooperate for six heartbeats. She gripped the desk until it passed.

She wrote neither fact down.

If she logged it, she’d be labeled compromised. If she was labeled compromised, she’d be removed from the work. If she was removed from the work, he stayed missing.

She packed her kit. Slate. Chalk. The burned scrap. The off-channel reports.

Tomorrow they left for Grindheim.

Tomorrow she followed a physics trail into territory where institutions had no grip and networks had all of it.

At the bottom of the stairs outside her workspace, she wrote one line in a private margin:

*Authority chose concealment. Physics will decide who’s lying.*

Her hand shook while she wrote it.

She still didn’t move the tremor into the official log.

[ASSISTANT]
import re
from pathlib import Path

orig = Path("/mnt/data/N02_Light_Cracks_Through_Ch1-12.md").read_text(encoding="utf-8")

def extract_chapters(md):
    # returns prefix and dict n->chapter text
    prefix, rest = md.split("## CHAPTER 1:", 1)
    chapters = {}
    # add back chapter1 marker
    md2 = "## CHAPTER 1:" + rest
    # split at next chapter headings while keeping delimiters
    parts = re.split(r'(?=\n## CHAPTER \d+:)', "\n"+md2)  # leading newline to match pattern
    for part in parts:
        if not part.strip():
            continue
        m = re.match(r'\n## CHAPTER (\d+):', part)
        if m:
            n = int(m.group(1))
            chapters[n] = part.lstrip("\n")
    return prefix, chapters

prefix, ch = extract_chapters(orig)
sorted(ch.keys())[:5], len(ch)

[TOOL]
([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 12)

[ASSISTANT]
def insert_after(text, anchor, addition, occurrence=1):
    idx = -1
    start = 0
    for _ in range(occurrence):
        idx = text.find(anchor, start)
        if idx == -1:
            raise ValueError(f"Anchor not found: {anchor[:50]}")
        start = idx + len(anchor)
    return text[:start] + addition + text[start:]

def insert_before(text, anchor, addition, occurrence=1):
    idx = -1
    start = 0
    for _ in range(occurrence):
        idx = text.find(anchor, start)
        if idx == -1:
            raise ValueError(f"Anchor not found: {anchor[:50]}")
        start = idx + len(anchor)
    # For before, use idx of last found
    idx = text.find(anchor)
    return text[:idx] + addition + text[idx:]

def wc(s): 
    return len(re.findall(r"\b[\w’'-]+\b", s))

sum(wc(ch[i]) for i in ch), wc(orig)

add2_a = """

Corlexi held the handle of the door for one extra heartbeat after it latched, not out of hesitation—out of measurement.

Salee didn’t waste a runner on courtesies. If she spent breath to tell him *she sees you*, it meant she wanted his next movement to be conscious.

He counted the room again. Table legs. Chair placement. The angle of the lamp bracket he’d just corrected. The thin dust line along the window sill that would show if someone listened from outside.

Nothing obvious.

That was the point.

He rebuttoned his shirt slowly. Not to hide the scars—anyone who would search him would find them anyway—but to force his posture back into the merchant shape. An embedded man survived by being boring.

Outside, the district vibration kept changing. Not a single strike, like the Brinehammer—this was continuous load. Footfalls. Door slams. Wet bundles dragged over stone. People arriving in waves and pretending it was a trickle.

He could feel the building disagree.

Stone columns took weight clean. The wood beams above them took it with a delay that read like reluctance.

He wrote, on the back of a shipping sheet where numbers were allowed to look ugly:

*Observation: network contact confirmed. Counter-observation probable. Maintain cover. Avoid clean interventions.*

Then he crossed the room and put his palm against the main wall seam.

Form gave him information if he listened the way the stone listened.

Not names. Not motives.

Pressure lines. Micro-shifts. The beginning of failure before anyone screamed.

The seam told him the first floor was already being asked to do more than it had been built for.
"""

add2_b = """

He watched the people move after his comment, the way a man watches water decide its path.

One woman shifted her bundle to the stone base without looking like she was obeying. A boy dragged a stool away from mid-span and told himself he’d had the idea first. Someone braced a door with a plank and pretended it was just “keeping drafts out.”

Salee’s network did that. It made compliance feel like choice.

A tall man near the stairwell—textile knot on his wrist—met Corlexi’s eyes for a fraction of a heartbeat and then looked away as if embarrassed by having *noticed*.

Corlexi filed it: *network witness present.* Not hostile. Not friendly. Just aware.

Upstairs, he rinsed his hands in a basin he didn’t need, because bodies did what minds could not confess.

In the rinse water, he saw copper dust cling to the skin at the cuticles—the residue that followed any precise hold, even one done without visible filaments. It wasn’t a cost marker. It was a trace marker.

He scrubbed harder than necessary until the water ran clear.

Then he went back to his table and made a merchant’s face at the numbers, as if that was the only thing in the world that could ruin him.
"""

“Because your ledger page wasn’t lost,” Corran said, low. “It was removed. I saw the request in archive before it got scrubbed. Administrative channel. Crown-level reach.”

[ASSISTANT]
add3_a = """

She didn’t trust a line just because it looked neat.

She checked angles twice. Measured the distances against street scale and remembered that Guild maps lied by omission—collapsed alleys weren’t removed, just unlabeled until someone important complained.

Each report got its own verification pass. Ink density. Stamp pressure. The way a clerk’s hand hesitated before writing “zero casualties,” as if the page itself might argue.

One report had been rewritten over an earlier number. The original casualty estimate still ghosted through the paper if she held it against the lamp.

Authority edited outcomes after the fact.

She logged that privately and kept the main slate clean.

When she was done, the map wasn’t a story. It was a constraint: every clean hold sat on the same implied path, as if someone were moving with purpose rather than responding to random collapse.
"""

add3_b = """

Corran’s eyes flicked once to the corner of the room where the corridor cameras couldn’t see. A habit from working under people who punished habits.

“They didn’t just revoke you,” he said. “They gave your authorizations a replacement. Crown co-mark. Relief routing. Stabilization protocol. Every inquiry now routes through a table with guards standing over it.”

He swallowed and forced the words out in one run, like ripping a bandage.

“They’re using the Brinehammer as justification to consolidate paperwork. Which means they consolidate bodies. Which means the minute you ask a question, you get added to a list.”

Tiffani’s chalk moved once, then stopped. She didn’t look at him.

“Your name is already known,” Corran added. “Not as a person. As a tool.”

Tools got assigned.

Tools got taken.
"""

add3_c = """

Bram didn’t argue after that. He shifted into his other competency—the one he hated admitting he had.

He traced his finger along the map and named routes the Guild didn’t list. Textile lanes. Harbor cut-throughs. Places where a pass meant less than a familiar face.

“My aunt runs a remnants wagon,” he said. “Grindheim gets cloth whether the Crown likes it or not. The escort pass will be in my name. You’ll be my ‘inventory assessor.’”

“That’s not a role,” Tiffani said.

“It’s a lie that sounds boring,” Bram replied. “Boring gets waved through.”

Tiffani copied the eight reports by hand anyway. Ink duplicated. Margins coded. The kind of redundancy that made burning a page insufficient.

She placed the duplicates in three different locations: inside her kit’s false bottom, inside Bram’s rolled cloth, and inside a seam of her coat where only a tailor would look.

Then she snapped the kit closed.

Tomorrow wasn’t a plan.

Tomorrow was a timing window.
"""

ch3_new = ch3
ch3_new = insert_after(ch3_new, "The geometry held.", add3_a)
ch3_new = insert_after(ch3_new, "Administrative channel. Crown-level reach.", add3_b)
ch3_new = insert_after(ch3_new, "Accepted.", add3_c)

wc(ch3), wc(ch3_new)

add4_a = """

He lay back down for one full breath and tried to remember what it felt like to be helpless.

The memory didn’t fit.

His legs felt like they belonged to him again, but the ownership was conditional—late responses, slight drift, the sense that his body was obeying through an intermediary he hadn’t hired.

He checked the room the way Tiffani would have: counts, marks, exits.

There was only one door. Old iron latch. Two hinge pins. The latch had been opened recently—oil smear at the catch—then wiped clean.

Someone wanted the door to look untouched.

He moved to the corner where the pipe
